heading: INT. ALAN'S OFFICE - SHORTLY BEFORE
content:
ALAN (60s) the owner of Cinema Four, is at his desk, cigarette in hand, scribbling into an accounting notebook. He's a worn man, with features bloated from years of financial stress.
Behind Alan is a large spread of old photos and newspaper clippings from the theater's glory days.
Sprawled across the small office, dejected after a long, grueling meeting is the theater's crew, who are all wearing the standard Cinema Four uniform: for the men, a brown suit with a white shirt and name tag with their position. For the women, a brown tweed skirt with long white socks and a white shirt.
The crew consists of:
JIMMY (30s), the theater's manager. Short, glasses, receding hairline, with an underwhelming stature. He stands closest to Alan, with a clipboard and street-cart coffee.
MARY (early 30s), the theater's curator. Bohemian and commanding, she's covered with tasteful poke and stick tattoos.
CONNIE (early 20s), usher, a flower child who's floaty and a true sweetheart. Keeping her hair up with a bandana, she's the only one sitting on the floor, legs crossed.
ROBERT (mid 20s), ticket seller, derives his look and demeanor from his heroes - Andrei Tarkovsky and Peter Bogdanovich. A filmmaker himself, with no credits, he wears a neck scarf and sunglasses. Strapped across his chest, ready for use at a moment's notice, is a vintage director's viewfinder.
WARREN (mid 20s), usher, portly and jolly. He stands beside Robert, his partner in filmmaking endeavors. He is holding the book
Notes on the Cinematographer
by Robert Bresson and a large soda from the concession stand.
CHARLOTTE (mid - late 20s), the theater's projectionist. She stands away from the table, almost separated from the rest of the crew. She has an effortless sophistication and natural beauty.
After a sustained silence, Connie shouts, exasperated.
CONNIE
Why don't we just rope it off?!
The idea hangs in the ether.
JIMMY
Connie, if you rope it off, then the second row
becomes
the first row.
Connie is unconvinced.
MARY
Our problem isn't the construct of the front row. We're talking about seat-to-screen
distance
.
Jimmy throws his arms out in complaint.
JIMMY
(frustrated)
Distance
is a construct. It's only relative to your perspective of the third dimension.
(turning to Robert)
Robert.
(he is unresponsive)
Robert!
(snapping)
How the hell is this a team meeting when half the room refuses to speak?
Warren, standing beside Robert, nudges him subtly. Behind his sunglasses, life flutters, Robert leans forward.
ROBERT
The devil's in the details, but so is salvation.
(he takes hold of his viewfinder)
You're far, but...
(looking into the viewfinder and zooming)
Now you're close. Who's to say where you really are?
Jimmy takes his glasses off and grips his eyes.
JIMMY
I'm going to stroke out.
ALAN
Jimmy, relax.
JIMMY
Relax?!
(he snatches a letter from Alan's desk)
"The Brooklyn Borough Small Claims Court has ruled in favor of the plaintiff. Cinema Four LLC must pay $1,607 dollars and 39 cents to the Levine family for injuries sustained during a fifteen hour screening of
Berlin Alexanderplatz.
"
(skimming)
"rampant negligence" ...  "past trauma" ... "the siege of Leningrad" ...
(scanning for the important part)
"Damages cover the purchase of nine AirFlow Max neck braces and $75 dollars per person in pain and suffering."
Robert sighs deeply.
ROBERT
(to Mary)
Wasn't this during
Shoah
?
MARY
It was a double feature.
JIMMY
How did nobody notice, for twenty five straight hours, nine...
litigious
individuals?
(glaring at Charlotte)
Charlotte! You didn't think that was a touch strange? An empty theater and a packed first row?
CHARLOTTE
(quick retort)
They could've been nearsighted or just wanted an immersive experience.
JIMMY
At least alert The General!
CHARLOTTE
Don't even try to bring this down on me! Besides, The General thinks he's fighting for a hill in South Pacific. You run this place, own up!
ALAN
(to Jimmy)
She's right.
JIMMY
(to Charlotte)
You and The General are the only ones who have eyes in the screening room. That's all I'm saying.
CHARLOTTE
So now I work the projection booth and security? Should someone print me a new badge? I do
my
job, and if you're going to parade him around as "security" -
JIMMY
(interrupting Charlotte)
Stop. Connie, can you go find The General please? He should be here to defend himself against this ableist slander.
Connie stands and scurries out of the office.
WARREN
(whispering to Robert)
Luckily, Charlotte can afford Ativan. Keep the crazy at bay, so she can have a solid lucidity about the world around her.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - CONTINUOUS
content:
Connie walks quickly through the lobby. Zev is where we last left him, peering through the glass entrance doors. When he sees her, he softly knocks in hopes of getting her attention.
Connie sees Zev and approaches the door. She takes a quick glance at her watch - 8:57am. She pulls a blind from above the door and walks away.
Zev is startled and stuck staring at a "CLOSED" sign. He tries the door, which is locked.

heading: INT. THEATER OFFICE - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Alan rests his cigarette in an ashtray. He sighs deeply and looks at his staff. The crew, including Jimmy, turn and wait for him to say something. Alan takes a moment, wiping his mouth with his hand in hesitation of delivering bad news.
ALAN
You know how much this hurts me... and - and I'm working on bringing someone in who's done this before and can bring us back to the golden days. I don't want be the boogeyman, but the lease is up at the end of the year and I have no idea if we'll be able to afford the new one. Definitely not by getting sued. Not to mention, we still have to replace poor Isaac.
(stopping to smoke)
Covering the neck braces comes out to $68.39 per head for the next six weeks.
The room is silent.
MARY
What about pain and suffering?
JIMMY
Jesus Christ, Mary, we're all suffering!
ALAN
No, no, he's right. You've all done more than enough for the Four, and you know how grateful I am.
MARY
(to Alan, kindly)
We share in profit AND we share in the losses. When things pick back up, you'll be stuffing our envelopes.
JIMMY
This place hasn't turned a profit in 15 years.
WARREN
(whispering to Robert)
Is this going to push back our shoot dates?
ROBERT
(sighing)
No... I'll have to go to Brooklyn Mirage this weekend and sell some tranq.
WARREN
(worried)
But, the lifetime ban!
ROBERT
It must be done.
CHARLOTTE
(despondent)
I'll pay... I should've been watching.
MARY
Don't you dare -
Connie comes back into the room, followed by Zev, who has his hands zip-tied behind his back. THE GENERAL follows closely into the office after him.
THE GENERAL, the theater's janitor and security, is a middle aged man dressed in a commander's uniform studded with military accolades and carrying a broom. He comes off as mentally unsound, possibly schizophrenic, either way unaware of reality.
THE GENERAL
I caught him scoping out the perimeter.
ZEV
(sheepish)
I -
CHARLOTTE
Oh, so he sees a customer standing outside six minutes before we open and he's got the guy in shackles?
THE GENERAL
(to Zev, a whisper audible to all)
She's about as dry as a new nun's nest.
There's a silence as the group examines Zev. He is uncomfortable receiving their collective gaze.
ZEV
I'm here for the concession stand job. Or Zaidenberg told my mother I should stop by... He said Alan would be expecting me.
Alan's eyes light up. He stands to greet Zev warmly.
ALAN
That would be me son. Come on in. Zaidenberg is a good man. Sold me a lemon once, but what's a lemon between friends?
(chuckling)
Alan walks over and warmly greets with him an outstretched hand, which cannot be reciprocated by Zev who is still detained.
ALAN
(to The General)
Free this man.
The General begrudgingly cuts the zip-tie with an army knife.
THE GENERAL
(muttering)
Big mistake. Big. Huge.
WARREN & CONNIE
(in unison)
Pretty Woman!
Warren and Connie giggle.
JIMMY
(to Zev)
Ever work a cinema?
ZEV
No. I used to work for my uncle's car dealership, but he recently went under -
WARREN
He has experience with failing business, seems like a perfect fit.
ZEV
(clearing his throat)
Under investigation.
WARREN
Ah. Well, still. Dark stuff.
Zev nods and squeaks out shy smile.
JIMMY
(ignoring Warren)
Ever work with food?
ZEV
A few summers ago, I was a delivery driver for Nathan's in Brighton.
WARREN
You're replacing
Isaac
with a guy who drove around in a hot dog shaped truck?
MARY
I kind of agree with that.
JIMMY
(to the crew)
But we don't know when he's coming back or if he'll ever even walk again.
ROBERT
That's Oscar Meyer, with the hot dog truck, not Nathan's. Nathan's is just a regular truck.
MARY
What?
The group looks at Zev for the truth.
ALAN
(to Zev)
Well?
ZEV
(gesturing to Robert)
He's right, it's just a painted U-Haul.
Mary begrudgingly accepts this information.
JIMMY
(to Alan)
What do you figure this does for the payments?
Alan thinks for a moment.
ALAN
Probably gets you down to about 17, 18 bucks a person.
Jimmy takes this as a strong point for Zev's employment.
JIMMY
(to himself)
Ooph, sweet cheddar...
(to the group)
Uh... I feel like standard protocol? Majority rules?
Alan nods.
ALAN
(to Zev)
What's your name son?
ZEV
Zev.
ALAN
All in favor of Zev here to join us, say aye.
JIMMY
(lukewarm)
Aye.
WARREN
(unenthusiastic)
Aye.
MARY
Nay, for nepotism.
CHARLOTTE
(doesn't care)
Aye.
CONNIE
(sweetly)
Aye.
WARREN
(shaking Robert)
Robert.
ROBERT
Aye, abstain.
ALAN
And the "ayes" have it.
(shaking Zev's hand)
Welcome to Cinema Four.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - LATER
content:
It's a slow afternoon at Cinema Four and the foyer is deserted, save for Zev, who is at the helm of the concession stand for his first day on the job.
Last renovated in the 1970s, the theater has been in permanent decay through years of diminishing business and financial strain, which is reflected in the threadbare carpets and kitsch decor.
Bookending the foyer are a glass concession stand at one end and a velvet couch, a relic from the cinema’s opening in the 1930s, at the other; a circular lounge area spreads out halfway between them. Extending from the lobby is a hallway leading to the screening room with a staircase to the balcony and projection booth.
Robert and Warren come as a pair behind the concession stand, not acknowledging Zev's existence, and pour themselves sodas from the fountain.
WARREN
Jerry was wrecked on set. I won't stand for that on my crew. He's a messy bastard and a filthy drunk.
ROBERT
He's been sober for 25 years; I've never even seen him sip a cup of coffee. And he's the best technician in town.
WARREN
Right, I forgot you're the perceptive one. You're a goddamn space cadet!
ROBERT
Yeah so were The Beatles.
WARREN
But you never saw their crew stumbling around turning up the amps to 11!
ROBERT
You just think he's a homophobe.
WARREN
I caught him reading a John Ford biography. He thinks John Wayne made a great Genghis Khan for Chrissake!
ROBERT
So what? Maybe he has eye for those western cuties. Tell me you don't.
WARREN
The searchers are basically genocidal reactionaries. How can he shoot our movie toting a 1950s vision of masculinity?
Mary comes into the theater with a large brown paper bag filled with lunches.
Jimmy exits Alan's office and walks towards Mary. Mary hands out subs to Warren, Jimmy, Robert, and Connie.
WARREN
Did you remember, no vinaigrette?
MARY
(to Warren)
Of course.
(to Connie)
Connie, sweetie, I texted you and  you never replied. They were all out of Soba and I knew you wouldn't eat cold cuts, so here's some French bread.
CONNIE
Oui oui.
Jimmy takes a couple of bites and scurries over to Zev.
JIMMY
Sorry, pal, where were we?
ZEV
Press the cup against the metal and the soda comes out.
ALAN (O.S)
(from across the lobby)
Jimmy!
JIMMY
Hey Mary, do you mind?
Mary walks behind the counter to join Zev. She picks up where Jimmy left off.
MARY
Okay... So, you've got your fountain, snacks, popcorn, pump, napkins, and straws. And we're cash only, but giving our fair share to the Uncle Sam is a pillar of what it means to work at Cinema Four. So, don't lose the receipts.
ZEV
(serious)
Understood.
MARY
Also we have a strict No Outside Snacks and Beverages policy. Before each showing, we search a few patrons at random.
(low voice)
Between you and me, it's always the seniors, but we aren't ageists.
Mary opens a cabinet under the stand revealing an assortment of snacks in a basket labelled "contraband".
MARY
After the show they can pick up whatever we confiscate, but they almost always forget. We put the good ones back up for sale.
Zev ruffles through the bin.
Two OLD GRANNIES walk into the theater. Mary glances at them.
MARY
(chuckling)
You know, when I picked
Salò
, I never thought we'd pack the house with golden girls.
Zev gulps as he watches Mary walk to the ticket counter and meet the old grannies. He surveys the lobby. Connie and Warren stand by the velvet rope that separate the lobby from the screening rooms.
The two women approach Connie. GRANNY #1 has a tote bag with soda and candy. GRANNY #2 has a walker with an IV drip. They hand their tickets to Connie, Warren stands by.
CONNIE
(to Granny #1)
Hi ladies, can I see some tickets?
Granny #1 has the tickets for the two of them. She hands them to Connie.
GRANNY #1
The theater sent me these, for my birthday.
CONNIE
Well happy birthday!
(looking at the ticket, it has an "x" marked on it)
Oop! Looks like I'm going have to conduct a snack search.
GRANNY #1
What?
CONNIE
Do you consent to be searched by this gentleman here?
GRANNY #1
Don't you dare touch me with your thorns.
WARREN
Thorns ma'am?
GRANNY #2
Horns, thorns! We don't want you hopped up looking for something sweet.
(gesturing to Connie)
She can do it.
Warren shoots a glance to Connie.
WARREN
Actually ma'am, I... enjoy men. She...
(gesturing to Connie)
enjoys men
and
women. So technically, she'd be the thorny one.
The grannies are visibly confused. Connie sees the situation getting sticky and quickly shuts it down.
CONNIE
Never mind! Enjoy the movie ladies. And happy birthday!
The grannies walk past them into the screening room.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - MINUTES LATER
content:
Zev sees the rest of the crew gathered off to the side on the velvet couch eating their sandwiches together.
MARY
I just don't know what we're going to do.
JIMMY
We did fine, seven tickets for a Tuesday evening. What do you want, a full house?
CONNIE
Two of them were comped. Birthday tickets.
JIMMY
Shit, Connie, we stopped the birthday tickets years ago.
Zev watches them chat, feeling alienated by himself at the stand. He looks down below the counter at a Ziplock bagged PB&J.
He takes a bite of the sandwich and gingerly pours himself a soda, looking around to see if anyone notices, but the crew are all busy bantering inaudibly to one another.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - MOMENTS LATER
content: The grannies are watching the movie. The granny with the IV drip removes the tube from her arm and puts it in her drink.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - LATER
content:
The lobby is calm.
Zev's elbows rest on the concession stand countertop; he's slowly adjusting to the slow rhythms of Cinema Four.
Within earshot, Robert and Warren are huddled around a payphone, sharing the receiver between them.
WARREN
Hello? Hi Mr. Galowitz thanks for joining the line - no we were just talking shop, we're ready when you are.
(listening)
Uh huh. Okay. Here's Robert.
Warren nudges Robert. Robert doesn't take the receiver.
ROBERT
(whispering)
Talk me up!
WARREN
(a touch frantic)
Robert, Robert, he's a great guy, he's the next big thing, what else can you say?
(away from the receiver to Robert)
What else can I say?
Robert snatches the receiver.
ROBERT
Hello? Mr. Galowitz?
(pause)
Yes, yes. Just to warn you - it's very literary - very expressive - a bit nebulous, so it can be hard to describe. It's about a kid who grew up in New Jersey. He's spent his entire life looking across the Hudson at the Manhattan skyline.
Warren pulls the phone in-between them.
WARREN
So close, yet so far.
ROBERT
Exactly. The only time his parents ever let him go into the city was on a school field trip, to the Met, in the late 90s. And that's the New York he fell in love with, the one from the 90s. And so he grows up, spending every day, every waking moment, fantasizing about the city.
Then he gets to be a little older, more independent, and he returns to the city.
WARREN
On the bus.
ROBERT
On the bus, that's right. He gets there and he hates it. Nothing's right, it's full of chain restaurants; a neon shit version of Disneyland. He feels such deep sadness at its decline, so he sets out to document the end of the city, to capture those last gleaming images of authenticity.
WARREN
And then it becomes like a documentary. It's an entirely new type of movie, not completely fiction, not completely documentary.
ROBERT
(to Warren)
I could've said it better myself.
(in receiver)
It's not even about characters, really. It's about motion, movement, sensation. Like
Koyaanisqatsi.
Robert looks confused.
ROBERT
Hello?
WARREN
Mr. Galowitz? It's Warren now.
(listening)
Yes, of course I know he doesn't have any credits. We discussed this - he's up and coming.
(putting the receiver to his chest)
He doesn't wanna invest, you're an unknown. It's too risky!
Robert furiously grabs the phone.
ROBERT
Do you know why you're going nowhere in life? Because you've got no imagination. This is like 1966 and you've got an Italian kid on the line by the name of Marty Scorsese - ever heard of him - and you "just don't see it working out." Good luck explaining this to your boss when we premiere.
Robert hangs up the phone emphatically. A beat of silence.
WARREN
(whispering)
He's the new president of Miramax.

heading: EXT. THEATER - AN HOUR LATER
content:
Zev walks out of the theater. He looks through the glass doors back into the lobby.
The crew are chatting jovially as Alan walks over to Jimmy. Alan whispers into Jimmy's ear, who nods along. Charlotte walks by the group to the theater entrance and Zev opens the door for her as she approaches.
CHARLOTTE
Thanks, have a good night. Sorry we didn't get a chance to talk much. If you start to lose your mind in the lobby, the screening room is a calming reprieve.
ZEV
Yeah, thanks, I'll check it out soon.
CHARLOTTE
Bye.
She walks to the curb into a black car waiting for her. Zev watches her be driven away and starts walking toward the subway. Jimmy pokes his head out of theater doors.
JIMMY
Hey, Zev!
Zev, startled, stops and turns around.
JIMMY
You got five?
ZEV
(unsure)
Yeah...
JIMMY
Come, let's get your uniform fitted. Tailor's right across the street.

heading: INT. TAILOR SHOP - MINUTES LATER
content:
Zev is on an elevated platform in front of a triple mirror getting measured by a TAILOR.
Jimmy is watching silently over his shoulder as the tailor continues measuring.
The tailor disappears to the back room and comes back with a tweed jacket.
Zev’s outfit is revealed: brown tweed suit paired with concession stand paper hat.
Jimmy smiles at Zev, who blushes. He turns and steps down.
Jimmy looks at him proudly and shakes his hand.
JIMMY
Welcome to Cinema Four.

heading: EXT. TAILOR SHOP - LATER
content:
Zev and Jimmy are outside. Jimmy lights a cigarette.
Zev stands by silently, unable to hide the newfound pleasure from Jimmy's acceptance.
ZEV
(extending for a handshake)
Well, I'll see you tomorrow. Thank you...
(glancing down at his outfit)
for everything.
JIMMY
(mid cigarette pull)
The hell are you on about? We're headed back to the theater. We've got takeout and drinks and the theater to ourselves.
ZEV
(jokingly)
We have the theater to ourselves most of the day... But I should be getting home, the sabbath starts soon. Another time, I promise.
Jimmy laughs.
JIMMY
No worries, enjoy.
He heads across the street toward the theater and Zev walks in the opposite direction.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - NIGHT
content:
On a quiet night in the theater, Zev stands behind the concession stand organizing a Mrs. Fields cookie display. He is clearly more comfortable at his post.
A jovial couple exit the screening room, the man pushes her up to the wall, blatantly ignorant to social norms, to give the woman an exaggeratedly romantic kiss. Zev can HEAR a French film playing inside.
Jimmy walks in, doing his rounds.
ZEV
(lifting the bin to Jimmy)
Contraband's running low.
JIMMY
Tell Connie to up the searches. I'll run to the bodega.
Jimmy turns away.
ZEV
Hey Jim -
(he turns back to Zev)
What's this movie all about? I can't understand a word.
JIMMY
You've never seen
Breathless?
Go catch the ending! It's a must-see.
Jimmy heads to the exit.
Zev removes his cap, places it on the counter, locks the register, and heads to towards the screening room.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - MOMENTS LATER
content: A crowd of about 12 PEOPLE sit in the dark theater, a mix of geriatrics who consider Jean-Paul Belmondo a contemporary and college-aged kids trying to impress their peers. A crevice of light spills in from the opening of the door to the lobby as Zev slips in. He walks past several empty rows and takes an aisle seat.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - 45 MINUTES LATER
content:
Zev is transfixed as the final notes of
Breathless
--featuring NEW YORK HERALD TRIBUNE--play. Zev remains glued as the aisle lights begin to warm up and the meager audience drifts toward the exit. Dazed, Zev looks around the theater, mumbling to himself as the last of the audience shuffles out.
The overhead lights burst on and moments later a voice is heard over the intercom.
CHARLOTTE (O.S)
Thank you for coming to Cinema Four. Please make your way to the exit, as the film has concluded. Enjoy the rest of your evening, and please come back again soon.
Zev gets out of his seat, and paces to the middle of the auditorium, looking up in the direction of the projection booth.
CHARLOTTE (O.S)
Oh!
(small laugh)
I was starting to worry we had another cadaver on our hands.
(playful)
Shawn, right?
ZEV
Zev!
CHARLOTTE (O.S)
I can hardly hear you. Come on up!
Zev points in the direction of the exit, looking for affirmation.

heading: INT. PROJECTION BOOTH - MOMENTS LATER
content:
The projection booth is cramped with boxes so old they could date back to the silent era. Green-walled and cavernous, the back consists entirely of projection equipment and the front has only a small desk atop which sits a lamp--the room's only light source--and various papers. On the wall surrounding the desk are movie schedules and various notes left from technicians and former projectionists.
After a knock on the door, Charlotte opens it and welcomes Zev in as if it were her home.
CHARLOTTE
Welcome! Come in! Don't mind the mess...
Charlotte is tidying up items from the table top.
ZEV
Sorry it took me a second... it's like dungeon of darkness coming up here.
CHARLOTTE
Those lamps died
years
ago. It's a miracle I've never taken a tumble.
ZEV
I'd catch you, but I'm not very strong.
There's an awkward silence.
ZEV
I didn't even realize there were seats up here.
CHARLOTTE
It's overflow for the orchestra, which has actually only happened when the basement flooded.
Charlotte unloads the reel from the projector. She shows Zev.
CHARLOTTE
Isn't she a beauty?
She lets him take a hold of it.
ZEV
Wow... I just caught the last half. What did he do? Seemed dramatic, he was stumbling for a while.
CHARLOTTE
You've never seen
Breathless
?
ZEV
I didn't even know it existed until about fifteen minutes ago.
CHARLOTTE
You're doing your life a disservice if you've never seen Godard.
ZEV
I haven't seen that one either. I like movies, but I haven't seen any of the ones you guys show here. A couple of years ago I saw
Reception,
which you probably showed
.
I don't remember much besides the buildings went like this.
Zev makes a bridge with his hands.
CHARLOTTE
(being sweet)
Inception
I think is what it was if I'm remembering correctly
.
We've never run it but Mary loves
Memento
.
ZEV
Yeah, we've been hanging out a bit,
with some of the others. They're very nice.
Charlotte grins. Theres a short silence.
ZEV
I asked about you.
CHARLOTTE
Oh really?
Zev nods.
CHARLOTTE
Mary thinks if you don't spend summers sweating in your studio next to the L train, you're missing out.
(switching the subject)
Anyway, you should try to catch this from the beginning, we're running it again tomorrow morning.
ZEV
I'd like that a lot.

heading: EXT. THEATER - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Zev and Charlotte exit the theater together. She locks up. Zev starts to walk away from the theater.
ZEV
What way are you headed?
CHARLOTTE
Oh, I'm waiting for my car.
(a pause)
Taking the train out all the way to the west side would take hours, it's just not worth it.
Zev shows some surprise.
ZEV
You come all the way to Bensonhurst for this?
CHARLOTTE
You'd be surprised, there are so few art house theaters still standing. I'd go practically anywhere besides Staten Island to find a booth.
Charlotte looks up at the theater marquee.
CHARLOTTE
It's really special Alan can keep this old bag alive. But come new year, it's probably the end. There's no way he'll be able to afford the lease renewal.
ZEV
Isn't there something we could do?
CHARLOTTE
It's not us, it's time.
There's a short silence.
ZEV
So you grew up in the city?
CHARLOTTE
Your typical Dalton girl.
ZEV
I guess that makes me your typical Benny boy.
Charlotte laughs.
CHARLOTTE
That's pretty good. I like it around here.
ZEV
Bensonhurst?
CHARLOTTE
It's just... life.
ZEV
It's changed so much, even since I was a kid. New buildings, new people, new stores. We have pilates now. It's pretty far out.
CHARLOTTE
Time rearing its head around again... and my dad actually built The Avalon Apartments.
ZEV
Oh...
CHARLOTTE
Yeah... since I started working at the Four, I've noticed the neighborhood start to shift and I keyed him in. He thinks this could be the next Bushwick - a real haven for artists.
A black car rounds the corner at the end of the street, headed towards the theater. As Charlotte heads to the car, Zev follows to open the door for her.
CHARLOTTE
If you liked
Breathless
you should check out
Vivre sa v
ie
. It's my favorite.
ZEV
Viv-ra say vee
?
CHARLOTTE
"My Life to Live."
ZEV
I'd like to think so.
Charlotte smiles, and takes a seat in the car.
CHARLOTTE
Goodnight, it was nice to formally meet you. Stop by the booth anytime; I mean it Benny boy.
Zev smiles and starts to close the door.
ZEV
Uh, where do I watch the movie?
CHARLOTTE
You can use my Criterion login. When you're through
Vivre
, just start going through the classics and you'll understand.
(to the driver)
Thomas, a pen?
Charlotte takes hold of Zev's arm and writes down her email and password on his forearm.
ZEV
Thanks.
The car takes off down the road. Zev watches it for a second, then turns and strides down the empty street.

heading: INT. ZEV'S ROOM - LATER
content:
Vivre s
a v
ie
is playing on Zev's TV. He looks befuddled, but intrigued.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - NIGHT
content:
The entire crew, minus Charlotte, is seated around the circular lounge in the center of the lobby. There are beers, bottles of wine, and boxes of Chinese take-out sprawled across floor. The crew are waiting patiently to begin the meal.
MARY
Zev, it’s tradition on nights like this for someone to say a few words. We either take stock of our privilege, mourn an injustice in the world, or just express gratitude that we’re alive. And as the newest member of the group, we’d like you to do the honors tonight.
ZEV
(blushing)
Let me think for a second...
ROBERT
He's stalling.
(friendly jab)
Just like Jimmy when we ask him to read one of his poems.
JIMMY
If I wanted to write
Milk & Honey
maybe I'd have something for you, but I'm working on my magnum opus.
ZEV
(ignoring Jimmy, stalling)
Should we wait for Charlotte?
CONNIE & WARREN
Oooooh.
ZEV
Do you guys not like her?
MARY
She's a Manhattan girl, and we're Brooklynites. There's nothing bad about that per se; not everyone wants to be authentic.
JIMMY
Speak for yourself. She cost us some serious cash. She's practically eviscerated Zev's first dozen checks.
ZEV
Huh?
JIMMY
(immediately)
Heads down everyone... Zev go ahead.
The rest of the group close their eyes and bow their heads. Zev takes a long pause and closes his eyes as well.
ZEV
(reciting softly)
Shalom aleichem, malachei hashareit, malachei elyon, mimelech malchei ham'lachim, hakadosh baruch hu. Boachem l'shalom, malachei hashalom, mal -
MARY
(ending the prayer short)
Ahmen!
Zev opens his eyes in surprise, having only done half the prayer. All of the crew are moving to fill their plates with food.
CONNIE
(exhale, deeply moved)
Wow, what was that?
ZEV
My torah portion, what I can remember at least. It's been a while since Hebrew school, but some of it just melds to your brain for life.
MARY
(quietly)
Indoctrination...
(to Zev)
What does it mean?
ZEV
Um, something like, "Peace be to you, ministering angels, messengers of the Most High, the supreme Sovereign, the Holy, and the Blessed One."
JIMMY
So you're an Israelite?
(a pause)
We're really sorry about... all that. We had the Great Depression, but, you, I couldn't imagine.
CONNIE
Yeah.
ZEV
Oh... no. It's fine. My family's been here for generations.
(trying to lighten the mood)
My great, great grandfather was the accountant for the Mayflower.
ROBERT
(impressed)
So you're a real Brooklyn guy.
WARREN
You don't really talk like one.
ZEV
The neighborhood is pretty yuppie now, so I never really picked it up. My parents have it.
MARY
Gentrifiers.
ROBERT
You must love
The Lords of Flatbush
.
ZEV
(taking a guess)
My parents were pretty strict with what I watched as a kid...
(hesitant)
If... you're ever free on a Friday night, my family does a dinner. You'd probably like my dad, he's more of that real
Brooklyn
type of guy you're talking about.
CONNIE
We'd love that.
The group agrees, then digs into the food.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - LATER
content:
Zev is standing in the concession stand with Robert, Warren, and Connie, pouring himself a drink from the soda fountain. Warren tops Zev's cup off with a healthy pour of cheap schnapps.
ROBERT
Stop stop stop. What you're saying to me is, you haven't seen
The Godfather
?
ZEV
I think I've probably seen it, but it was years ago. I remember a horse's head and guy with these big cheeks -
ROBERT
(laughing)
And who was he?
ZEV
The Godfather. I'm not sure of who though, do we ever find that out?
ROBERT
(playful)
No it's a big secret. Do you remember his name?
ZEV
I can't remember. Something with a "D"? Donald maybe?
ROBERT
Yes, Donald, exactly! Are you sure you haven't seen it recently? And he has a son.
ZEV
His son in the movie or real life?
ROBERT
(sarcastic)
In real life, Marlon Brando yeah. No, in the movie!
ZEV
I'm sorry, I don't remember him as a major character.
ROBERT
You have to watch Part 2 for that.
ZEV
There's two of them?
Warren and Connie are giggling.
CONNIE
Three, if you can believe it.
WARREN
The film community would disagree.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - LATER
content:
Connie, Warren, Robert, and Jimmy are in the main screening room of the theater.
Fast Times at Ridgemont High
is playing on the projector, without sound, and they are dancing to the soundtrack, which is PLAYING over the loud speaker.
The scene from the movie in which Cinema Four is referenced comes on screen and the entire crew CLAP and CHEER.
Jimmy stops dancing, skips to the screening room door, and pokes his head out. The MUSIC spills into the lobby.
Mary and Zev are cleaning up the food and drink.
JIMMY
(calling to them)
Get in here!
Mary and Zev, with trash bags in their hands look over.
MARY
Oh I love this song.
(she puts down the bags)
Let's go.
She tugs Zev's arm, who puts down his trash bag and gets pulled to the screening room. Zev is reluctant at first, but Mary gives him another tug.
MARY
C'mon, you're one of us.
Zev smiles and follows her, disappearing behind the screening room door. The MUSIC can still be heard, now more quietly, in the empty lobby.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - EVENING
content:
Zev is behind the counter placing a few new boxes of snacks under the glass. Warren is lying on the lobby couch reading through a script. Connie walks up to the counter.
CONNIE
It's Mary's birthday tonight, we're going to throw a surprise party at her place. We're meeting Jimmy at Party City.
ZEV
I'm not sure I can hang tonight.
CONNIE
What?! You have to!
(leaning in a little)
We got her a cake.
ZEV
Oh, well -
WARREN
(still reading)
You can't miss a birthday.
ZEV
I don't have a gift or anything.
WARREN
Volunteer for the photo wall, if you have an inkling for exhibitionism.
ZEV
(slightly confused)
Alright... let me close out the register and I'll meet you by the train.
CONNIE
(smiling)
Okay deal!
Connie and Warren walk towards their cubbies together as Zev watches them disappear. He then frantically fills and fastens a lid to a large soda cup and rummages for a bag of Finnish Fish beneath the counter, ensuring no one is watching him. Confident that the coast is clear, he slips out from behind the counter and up to the projection room.

heading: INT. MARY'S APARTMENT, LIVING ROOM - NIGHT
content:
All the lights are off.
Party favors half-assedly hang from the walls of the apartment.
The group is sprawled across the living room in various hiding spots.
Zev and Connie are crouched down behind the couch. Behind them, on the wall, are a set of photographs. They are of male's thighs and penises. Zev is examining them with Connie. They are speaking in whispers.
CONNIE
She's really got an eye.
ZEV
Mary took those?
Jimmy overhears them talking and scoots over to them from his hiding spot.
JIMMY
I came up with the idea.
CONNIE
It's brilliant.
JIMMY
When's the last time you went to the Met and saw a room full of Richards? Never. And when you finally see David, he's got the pecker of a school boy. I'm no art historian but I'd be real surprised if Michelangelo's ever painted flat-chested woman.
ZEV
I've been to the Met on a middle school trip, I remember they had these little turkey sandwiches that we got for free.
JIMMY
If we want to get past the way  bodies are objectified in society we need to destroy the phallic fallacy.
ZEV
Where does Mary find her subjects?
CONNIE
Friends and family mostly. That's Jimmy and... that's Robert. She'll probably ask you at one point, once you're more comfortable.
Mary’s key scrapes against the lock and the door lurches open.
CONNIE
Ooh shh! She's coming!
Mary comes into the living room and flips the lights on.
JIMMY, ZEV, CONNIE, WARREN, ROBERT
Surprise!
The group blows KAZOOs and POPs confetti. Mary jumps with surprise and glee.

heading: INT. MARY'S APARTMENT, LIVING ROOM - LATER
content:
Mary is sitting on the floor, wearing a birthday sash and tiara. Warren is next to her, adorned in party favors. Robert and Jimmy have some tassels and other birthday gear. Zev is favor-less, as he eats a slice of pizza.
MARY
All I'm trying to say is that in a profit-driven society, your brain doesn't get a chance to ponder the bigger questions of existence. If we equalized society, then we could be free to come into ourselves and create real art.
JIMMY
(argumentative, to Mary)
Equality is just not equipped to produce art. Art is a response to the ills of society. Imagine a world without crack cocaine. What would Keith Haring have done with that mural? What's Mapplethorpe up to without AIDS? In your utopia, we'd all be doing claymation.
CONNIE
(under her breath, to Zev)
Wait until he discovers the Brothers Quay.
Zev nods in agreement.
JIMMY
(at Connie)
I
know the Quay! And there's no way, the Quay use clay! Stick to
Chicken Run.
Mary's eye's perk up.
MARY
The pies!
She runs into the kitchen.
ROBERT
What about the Soviet Union? A completely classless society. Minds were able to ponder and wonder without the burden of
ego
. All they had was the
id
.
That's the world that gave us Andrei Tarkovsky, the greatest poet to ever point a lens. He didn't make films to sleep with hookers in the Hollywood Hills.
JIMMY
What a crock of shit. The only thing they were looking for in the "zone" was a goddamn Chase Bank.
Mary rushes back into the living room holding two apple pies.
MARY
The hard truth is that a man sees the female artist and thinks, "wow, we're going to have so much to talk about after sex". And a woman sees the male artist and thinks, "wow we're going to have so much to talk about during dinner."
JIMMY
What about Van Gogh? Poor guy wasn't hot until he was six feet under for a century. He was so horned up by the end, he chopped his ear off just to feel something.
MARY
He was a handsome one too. Imagine,
Starry Night
on an easel in his bedroom.
(she shudders with glee)
JIMMY
So you're trying to say a painting gives you more pleasure than the saps you drag back here? That
acrylic
is dry, baby.

heading: INT. MARY'S APARTMENT, FOYER - LATER
content:
It is later in the evening. Warren is grabbing his coat. Zev is passing through from the kitchen.
ZEV
You're headed out?
WARREN
I'm headed up for a smoke on the roof.
(to the living room)
Anyone game?
JIMMY
Absolutely.
CONNIE
I don't want to smoke a full one, Robby, can we split?
ROBERT
I was going to sprinkle some dust on mine, but you're free to ride along.
WARREN
(to Robert)
Don't go to Mars, please, we're supposed to run lines after this.
ROBERT
(laughing)
Relax, we already did lines.
Everyone besides Mary groups in the foyer. Zev turns back to Mary as the group is halfway out the door. He pauses in the doorway.
JIMMY
(to Zev)
You're not coming?
ZEV
Mary you're not coming?
MARY
No babe, don't worry about it.
ZEV
I don’t want to leave Mary alone here.
MARY
No it's alright, that's sweet of you for asking. I don't have the vice privilege, not even second-hand.
Zev stares blankly at her.
MARY
Take a billionaire and a man sleeping on the street. When the rich man gets stressed or tired or wants some euphoria, he can get a massage or go to Mohegan Sun. Have you ever seen a homeless person at Mohegan Sun?
ZEV
I've never been to a Mohegan Sun.
MARY
The poor man's options for a smile are dope, cigarettes, and cheap alcohol. All of which are detrimental. The rich man's "vices" are all beneficial, or at the very least, in his control. And therefore, it's completely absurd that those of us that are aware of our privilege would engage in vices reserved for those less fortunate.
ZEV
But don't we all drink?
MARY
Do you know how many antioxidants are in a glass of red wine?
Zev stares at Mary, unsure.
MARY
Really, it's fine.
Zev is uneasy but follows the rest out of the door, leaving Mary alone in the foyer. She starts cleaning up.
EXT. MOVIE THEATER - MORNING
Zev is standing outside the theater, waiting.
A black Mercedes pulls up and Charlotte gets out of the car.
Zev lights up as she meets him by the entrance.
ZEV
Hey!
CHARLOTTE
Oh hey - no keys?
She smiles and unlocks the door.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - CONTINUOUS
content:
The two stop at the crossroads between the concessions counter and the projection booth. Charlotte turns to Zev.
CHARLOTTE
If you like French New Wave... oh, I know,
The 400 Blows
. That's essential viewing.
ZEV
(with a smile)
I'm on it. Sorry, I'm still using your account, I watch something new almost every night.
(mimicking Peter Sellers)
"There's no fighting in here, this is the war room!"
Charlotte laughs, touching his arm softly.
CHARLOTTE
Classic, you're a natural.
(talking low)
You know, tonight's last movie ends pretty early. If you don't have anything going on, I could play
The 400 Blows
for you here. It really deserves the silver screen.
ZEV
I'd like that a lot.
Charlotte waves goodbye and heads to the stairs, up to the balcony.
Zev gets behind the counter and puts on his concession stand hat. He looks excited.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - LATER
content:
Zev stands behind the concession stand, anxious, sporadically looking at a clock on the wall as it ticks towards closing time. He counts the night's earnings, continuously losing track and having to start over.
The crew get packed up and begin heading home. Zev, still fiddling with the takings from the stand, perks up at the prospect of everyone leaving.
JIMMY
Cya, kid.
ZEV
Not hanging out tonight?
JIMMY
Nah, Mary's ducking out east to her family's place. And I've got a date. Been a while, so throw me a mazel if you've got any extras. We'll be back at it next week though.
Zev nods, hiding his pleasure at the news.
They say GOODBYEs and all exit the theater, leaving Zev alone.
A NOISE by the stairs indicates Charlotte is descending from the projection room, but her arrival in the lobby coincides with The General also arriving from the screening room.
CHARLOTTE
(whispering)
Get down!
Zev quickly hides behind the concession counter as The General emerges into the lobby with a broom and pale.
THE GENERAL
The hoi polloi have left the building!
CHARLOTTE
(to The General)
I'm just about to lock up...
He jumps into a salute and heads to the door. At the entrance he stops and turns back to Charlotte. He pulls out his flashlight.
THE GENERAL
Perimeter duty. We can't let the Kong breach the hill. Not on my watch.
He exits the theater.

heading: EXT. THEATER - CONTINUOUS
content:
The General walks away from the theater pointing his flashlight at various objects and people.
He flashes around a bus stop, ridden with graffiti and profanity.
THE GENERAL
(squinting)
Hm... hieroglyphics...
He takes out a field notebook and begins to scribble down what he sees.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - CONTINUOUS
content:
Zev crawls out from behind the concession stand. Charlotte is standing near him, smiling.
ZEV
He's quite the wildcard, makes me nervous.
CHARLOTTE
He's
the
case study on the dangers of method acting.
ZEV
He's playing a character?
CHARLOTTE
In 1990, The General scored an audition for Malick's comeback film,
The Thin Red Line.
It was gonna be his big break, so The General went full method. But, 1990 became '91, then '92, '93, '95, and goodbye. The General became The General for good. I don't even know his real name. Neither does he, anymore.
ZEV
(standing up)
Do you think he saw me?
CHARLOTTE
He would've called in an airstrike by now. I'm going to run and get set up. Can you turn off the house lights?
Zev watches her jog up the stairs and moves to the light switches, killing them all and shrouding the lobby in darkness, lit only by the radiance of the soda machine. He fixes each of them a soda.
DANKE SCHOEN begins to play, softly, over the theater's speaker system. Before he heads up the stairs, he fills two small bags with stale popcorn, douses them with Flavacol, and tucks a box of candy in his back pocket.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, BALCONY - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Zev jogs to the top of the stairs, and pauses a minute to catch his breath. He walks up to the projection booth door and gives it a KNOCK. A second later the door opens, Charlotte inside fiddling with the projection equipment and holding the door open with her foot.
ZEV
(smiling)
Refreshments.
CHARLOTTE
Thank you. Get comfortable.
Charlotte removes her foot from the door and it begins to fall closed.
ZEV
Oh are you not --
The door SLAMS shut in his face.
Zev proceeds down the balcony steps, taking a seat on the top row, directly under the projector. He sits down and gets comfortable. He sneaks a few glances up at the projector window to see if Charlotte is looking at him. She is not. The projector clicks on and the OPENING NOTES of
The 400 Blows
begin to play.
Zev's face reveals disappointment that he'll be viewing alone. He settles in to watch and about 15 seconds later the projection booth door is heard opening and closing. Zev looks to his right and sees Charlotte descending the steps to where he's sitting, taking the seat next to his.
They watch the opening of the movie. As Zev peeks at Charlotte he sees her doing the same. He keeps his eyes forward, rubbing his sweaty palms on his trousers.
Charlotte crosses her legs, which causes her foot to brush Zev's calf. She doesn't move it away.
After a moment of consideration, Zev grabs her hand. She squeezes back, and as the soundtrack to the film crescendos they both settle in to watch the movie, hand-in-hand.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, BALCONY - AN HOUR AND A HALF LATER
content: As the final notes of the film play, Zev and Charlotte are arm-in-arm, making out. The sounds of the ocean from the film play in the background.

heading: INT. THEATER OFFICE - AFTERNOON
content:
The entire staff is in Alan's office. Zev and Charlotte are seated next to each other.
DAKOTAH (early 20s) tall, handsome and tan, wearing a suit, walks in. He and Mary's eyes meet, and Mary is thrown off momentarily.
He smiles at the group, and then walks to Alan's side.
ALAN
Everyone, the dark days are over.  Remember I told you I was working on bringing in some operational expertise? Well, here he is!
He stands to shake Dakotah's hand.
ALAN
We've been selected as an "at risk business" for Dartmouth's MBA Consultancy Practicum. They've graciously sent us one of their best...
(looking down at a note)
Dakotah is "nearly at the top of his cohort, majoring in the business of entertainment." He's here to get to know us and give us some ideas for the future. He's nobody's boss, but he's got my ear, and I'm listening.
DAKOTAH
(embracing Alan)
Thank you for the kind words. It's truly a privilege to come here and work with you all. Before I dive in, I want to address the elephant in the room. It's never been an issue in the past, and I don't expect it to be one now...
(pausing to look over the staff and smiles)
I can already see the gears turning... "he looks familiar, where do I know that face?" The answer is
A Place Under The Sun -
I am the grandson of Montgomery Clift. Now I want to be clear, that does not mean I can call up Monty to come save the theater; he's dead. I do things on my own accord, and I'm going to turn this place around with sound business practices, hard work, and a bit of help from all of you.
There's a short pause, then it's obvious that he has finished his speech. Alan claps and the rest of the staff halfheartedly join in.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - LATER
content:
Post-meeting, Alan is giving Dakotah a tour of the lobby. Dakotah is holding a clipboard. Alan leads Dakotah over to Zev at the concession stand.
ALAN
Here's our other newbie, less than a year, doesn't feel like it huh?!
(chuckling)
Maybe Zev has some interesting thoughts for you.
DAKOTAH
That's great.
(handshake with Zev)
Dakotah Clift.
ZEV
Zev Stern.
DAKOTAH
(chummy)
You gotta show me the ropes Zev. I see the stand as a big part of our success. What we have here is a closed system, like an airport. Once the customer is in, you're their only source of nourishment. We crank up the heat and fix the Slurpee machine, I can already see massive potential.
Alan is clearly a big fan of Dakotah, very impressed.
ZEV
We have a "no outside snacks" policy and we conduct random searches at the rope. They're not so random really.
DAKOTAH
(to Alan)
Psh! This guy. You don't need me here! Business tycoon.
ALAN
It's kismet!
Jimmy walks through the lobby. Alan sees him and jumps to chat with him.
ALAN
Jim! Hold on a second.
(to Dakotah and Zev)
Talk, talk, I'll be back.
Alan leaves the two of them. As he walks away, Dakotah turns to watch the lobby with his back against the concession counter.
DAKOTAH
(taking in his new domain)
Starving artists. Well, not you, Zev. You’ve got it all figured out back here.
ZEV
I'm not an artist, Robert and Warren are working on a film, Jimmy's a poet, Mary's done some phenomenal portraits, and I'm not 100% sure what Connie does.
DAKOTAH
Don't sell yourself short.
(he turns to face Zev)
You've got...
something
. I could feel it in that room. Leadership. You just need vision, passion, and drive, and your wildest dreams will follow.
Zev's drawn in by Dakotah's charm.
ZEV
(blushing)
Yeah, maybe. I've gotten pretty into cinema myself. Not as much as the rest of the crew, but -
DAKOTAH
(turning back toward the lobby)
Imagine Vanderbilt, laying in bed.
(a baron's accent)
"There's going to be a railroad running through every city worth its salt!"
(back to normal)
Then he woke up, and he built it; Dreams to reality.
ZEV
(not fully buying it)
I think it was mostly the Chinese who built the track, he just paid for it.
Dakotah whips around to face Zev.
DAKOTAH
Exactly! And look at them now, taking over the world! Impressive stuff.
Dakotah leans back to survey the lobby.
He places a hand over Zev's hands on the concession counter.
DAKOTAH
This place is a lump of coal... and I'm going to make it a diamond.

heading: INT. ZEV'S HOUSE, LIVING ROOM -  AFTERNOON
content:
MRS. SHERYL STERN (60s) with heavy makeup, pearl earnings, and a fur coat is sitting on the couch in the living room watching television. The room is modestly kitsch, unchanged from the 1970s.
The doorbell RINGS. Sheryl immediately props herself up, wanting to look good for her incoming guests. No one immediately goes to open door.
SHERYL
(yelling)
Shlomo! Get the door!
Zev comes through the hallway to the door.
He opens it, revealing Jimmy, Robert, Connie, Warren, and Mary waiting outside. They are all dressed nicely. Jimmy is holding a plastic-wrapped shrimp cocktail.
ZEV
I can't believe you actually showed up...

heading: INT. ZEV'S HOUSE, DINING ROOM - MINUTES LATER
content:
An extended dining table is covered with a beautiful patterned tablecloth. Around the periphery sit the crew, the men in outsized and obviously borrowed yarmulkes. At the head of the table sits Sheryl, flanked by her son and her husband SHLOMO STERN (60s) in a white dress shirt half buttoned, with a tank top and star of David chain visible underneath. He has a traditional New York-Yiddish hybrid accent and talks exuberantly with the crew while his wife breaths in wine. There is one seat and place setting next to Zev that is empty.
MARY
(to Mr. / Mrs. Stern)
Again, we're terribly sorry about the shrimp cocktail, we had no idea. It was so inappropriate, we feel terrible.
SHLOMO
Nonsense! You were never briefed! Zevvy has always had that special  non-attention to detail.
The crew slurp up every last facet of their environment, excited to be in surroundings so different from their own. Zev, meanwhile, pouts quietly on the sidelines, embarrassed and regretful for inviting everyone.
SHERYL
Start already, they're starving!
SHLOMO
Alright, alright.
Shlomo stands.
SHLOMO
I hope Zev told you, we don't do the rituals much anymore, but he told us "give them the authentic experience!" It's Sunday, not Friday, so we've already missed the mark on that, but no worries, in about thirty minutes, Zevvy will be crying, she'll be off the 'shevitz, and I can give you all a ride to the J.
ZEV
Dad, please.
SHLOMO
Okay, first we light the candles. We're just going to use the menorah because we couldn't find a candle holder, but this is what we use on
Hanukkah so it's all still very Jewish. Don't worry.
Shlomo lights two candles in the menorah.
SHLOMO
(to the crew)
Like this -
He waves his hands in front of his face three times and covers his eyes. Everyone mimics him.
SHLOMO, SHERYL, ZEV
(singing)
Barukh atah Adonai Eloheinu melekh ha’olam asher kid’shanu b’mitzvotav v’tzivanu l’hadlik ner shel shabbat
.
A moment of silence.
ZEV
You can open your eyes.
The crew is thoroughly enjoying the show.
CONNIE
That was beautiful.
SHERYL
Zevvy, sing
Shalom Aleichem
with your father. It's been years, it's a special night.
ZEV
Mom, please -
ROBERT
We want to hear it, Zevvy.
JIMMY
C'mon!
Zev resigns. He gets up and stands next to his father.
SHLOMO
We are welcoming the angels to our home, to our dinner, on this beautiful night together... one, two, three,
(silently mouths "four")
SHLOMO, ZEV
(singing)
Shalom aleichem mal’achei hashareit mal’achei elyon.
Mimelech malchei...
The doorbell RINGS, interrupting the singing.
No one gets up to get it and the crew look at Shlomo, Zev, and Sheryl, confused as to why no one is answering the door. The Sterns stare back blankly
.
A few moments pass.
SHLOMO
(to the air)
Oh I'd love for a bit of a breeze. I wish just by chance maybe the door would open. If only the door would fly open by some sort of happenstance.
The crew is confused.
ZEV
(whispering, to the crew)
We can't work after sundown.
SHLOMO
(to the air)
Oh how I'd just love for a nice
gentile
breeze to open the door.
There's a silence. Warren gets up uncertainly
WARREN
I guess I can get it.
SHLOMO
(to the air)
Oh thank you so much, what a great idea you thought of. Bless you, mazel my dear boy!

heading: INT. ZEV'S HOUSE, FOYER - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Warren opens the door to see Charlotte, holding a bottle of wine, and a taxi driving off behind her.
CHARLOTTE
(sweetly)
Hi.
WARREN
(surprised)
Oh, hi... I didn't know you were on the guest list.
Charlotte walks past him inside.

heading: INT. ZEV'S HOUSE, DINING ROOM - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Charlotte enters the dining room followed by Warren.
Sheryl is passing out bowls of matzah ball soup.
MARY
(surprised)
Woah!
The crew look up to see Charlotte. They are stunned. They look at Zev.
CHARLOTTE
(to her hosts)
I'm sorry I'm late. I'm Charlotte. Thank you so much for having me.
(handing the bottle to Shlomo)
It one of my father's favorites.
Charlotte holds the bottle out in front of her.
SHLOMO
(to Charlotte)
Oh, what a lovely gift! Thank you, thank you! Shlomo Stern. A pleasure, please sit.
Charlotte takes her seat in the empty chair next to Zev.
SHERYL
(to Charlotte)
Here you are sweetie, I'll get you a soup. How many balls?
CHARLOTTE
Uh... two please.
Mary exchanges looks with Jimmy. She pours him and herself glasses of wine, and then hovers over Charlotte's glass.
MARY
I'm sure it's not as good as what you brought.
CHARLOTTE
That's quite alright.
Mary pours her a full glass, to the brim.
Sheryl comes back with the soup and places it front of Charlotte.
MARY
(mockingly)
Just like Sadelles.
Jimmy smiles slyly at the joke. Charlotte does not dignify it with a response. Robert, Warren, and Connie are exchanging looks.
SHLOMO
I grew up with Benny Sadelle. We used to cut school together and catch a matinee at The Avalon. In the morning, he would stop at the Respoli's, get a bag of rainbow cookies - and it wasn't about a movement back then, that's just how they made them. He'd come into my bed with his feet at my head, completely kosher, and we'd sleep until about three or four. Then we would walk down to 15th Street and watch a picture
and eat our rainbow cookies. I remember when I was 12, I snuck in to
Bullitt
. With Steve McQueen, I bet you kids don't even know that movie.
ROBERT
(interrupting)
1968, Peter Yates.
SHLOMO
(surprised)
No, I'm almost certain it was McQueen...
(continuing his story)
But, I'll never forget there was an Albanian woman in the back of the theater who would stroke your thigh for a nickel, and for a dime she'd rub your back and say she was proud of you... I miss the old 'Bush. It was all candy stores, delis, and dentists. Now its coffee shops, Korean foot spas, and coffee shops. Who needs so much coffee and acupuncture?
MARY
(looking at Charlotte)
I heard The Avalon was beautiful, it's a shame they tore it down and built that horrid apartment complex.
Zev sees Mary looking at Charlotte. Charlotte doesn't flinch, staying completely composed and silent. She affectionately rubs Zev's hand under the table.
SHLOMO
On Tuesday nights, they had an open mic in the lobby, and me, Benny Sadelle, and Willy Katz were a trio called "Home Before Sundown".
JIMMY
It must've been incredible back then.
SHERYL
(slightly slurring)
It was a complete bore! Everything fun was in Manhattan.
SHLOMO
What'd do you know about fun?
SHERYL
If you'd made anything of yourself you wouldn't be rotting here, yapping on about all you did with those boys. They became successful men, and you?
SHLOMO
A father to a beautiful boy!
SHERYL
(ignoring his comment)
Do you think they dream about you as much as you do of them?
Shlomo retracts, his demeanor shifts from happy and friendly to genuinely upset. There is a long, uncomfortable silence at the table.
CHARLOTTE
(coming to his defense)
Woody Allen is from Flatbush.
Shlomo lets out a WAIL.
MARY
(fed up)
You mean that pervert who made a movie called
Manhattan
?
SHERYL
(nostalgic)
Oh, I miss Heywood. He had
something
, we knew it since the beginning. You have your dreamers
(gesturing to Shlomo)
And your doers. He was both.
Shlomo abruptly gets up from the table. He looks at his guests.
SHLOMO
(composing himself)
Shabbat Shalom.
He walks out of the room. The air is awkward. Sheryl gulps down her wine.
CONNIE
Shabbat Shalom.
CHARLOTTE
(concerned)
Was that -
SHERYL
(upset)
Don't mind him, sweetie, he had his chances...
(blushing, teary-eyed)
So did I.
CHARLOTTE
I feel awful.
MARY
You bring up a monster during a religious ceremony and think it's going work out?
JIMMY
Mary.
MARY
What?
Sheryl abruptly gets up from the table.
SHERYL
(quivering)
Shabbat Shalom.
She abruptly exits. An awkward silence falls on the room.
JIMMY
(to Zev)
Should we leave?
ZEV
No, no. We have to at least finish the soup. Here...
He gets up and goes over to a record player in the living room. He puts in a vinyl of a Hebrew album. It starts to PLAY. He returns to his seat and restarts eating his soup. The group follows, eating and listening to the music without speaking.

heading: INT. ZEV'S HOUSE, FOYER - LATER
content:
Jimmy, Robert, Mary, Connie, Warren, Charlotte, and Zev are at the door. All but Zev and Charlotte have their coat on.
Jimmy places his hand on Zev's shoulder.
JIMMY
Thank's for having us. Really, it was a pleasure.
ZEV
Sorry for this, I should've known.
Robert pats Jimmy on the back.
ROBERT
No way man, it was an experience.
Zev smiles meekly. He opens the door for them and all but Charlotte go outside. Jimmy notices Charlotte still inside.
JIMMY
(to Charlotte, trying to be nice)
Should we wait for your car?
ZEV
I'm going to drive her in when traffic settles down.
Jimmy's eyes flash between the two of them.
JIMMY
We'll see you two tomorrow then.
Jimmy gives Zev a look and then a mock salute before Zev closes the door. He jogs to catch up with the rest of the crew.
JIMMY
What the hell was that?
MARY
(resigned)
Shabbat shalom. Fuck me. Zev must've told her there was some special babka waiting.
WARREN
(gesturing to Robert)
We gotta catch the bus, we're supposed to be on set in thirty.
JIMMY
What set?
ROBERT
(stoic)
My apartment.
JIMMY
You're still working on Yobification? Hasn't it been like four years?
ROBERT
(irritated)
Hold on let me give you Linklater's number, he can tell you a thing or two about how long it takes to make a masterpiece.
The group reaches an intersection, Robert and Warren heading in one direction and the rest of the crew in another.

heading: INT. ZEV'S HOUSE, DINING ROOM - LATER
content:
Zev and Charlotte sits with arms entwined, utterly oblivious to anything except one another.
ZEV
I should've known...
CHARLOTTE
It was my fault.
ZEV
My mom hates that I took the job at the theater. She say's there's no future in scooping popcorn.
CHARLOTTE
It's never too late to make something of yourself. Manoel de Oliveira didn't make it until his 70s. Unfortunately not everyone can be Orson Welles.
ZEV
When did he make it?
CHARLOTTE
Well... he happened to make the greatest film of all time at 25. But you don't need to make the greatest anything. You should do whatever takes your mind off the fact that our world is filled with genocides, our farms with pesticides, and your parents don't love each other anymore.
ZEV
(looking Charlotte in the eyes)
I don't think of any of those things when I'm with you.
She smiles and stares back at him. They kiss.
ZEV
Do you know it's a mitzvah -

heading:
TITLE CARD:
OCTOBER
content:
INT.  THEATER LOBBY - AFTERNOON
Dakotah is standing next to Alan as NATHAN, a chubby boy in his early 20s with high functioning Aspergers and an Oakland Athletics hat, replaces movie posters in the lobby, which show the current slate of the theater.
Zev watches from the concession stand as Mary, Jimmy, Warren, Robert and Connie walk into the theater carrying lunch. Mary, holding a large brown paper bag, drops it immediately as she lays eyes on Nathan and his armful of posters.
MARY
What the fuck?! Why is money-ball touching my slate??
DAKOTAH
Marianne, be nice. This is my protégé Nathan, a close confidant of mine from Dartmouth. He's got just the plan to give us a boost in Q4.
Everyone besides Nathan registers the tension in the air.
NATHAN
(casually)
With Dakotah's help, I ran a SWOT analysis on the theater's most profitable demographic. Starting Monday we are going to run:
Midnight Cowboy
,
Beau Travail
,
Brokeback Mountain
,
Fox and His Friends
, and a rotating hit from the Marvel Universe.
WARREN
Now that's a lineup.
DAKOTAH
(to Alan)
What did I tell you?
MARY
(trembling)
This is a joke right? Alan? Robert? Anyone? Marvel? Should we just bend over and let you fuck us, or how do you want to do this?
ALAN
Mary, please, you're still our curator, you always will be, but we want a fresh pair of -
DAKOTAH
(cutting Alan off)
He's a genius Mary, trust me. We still want to hear your thoughts, but hear Nathan out. He's really onto something revolutionary.
NATHAN
Uh, well my research shows young, closeted jock homosexuals in their early teens struggle with distinguishing other gays from the rest boys on the team. So many mixed signals in the locker room, it's impossible to tell when a pat on bum or a light kiss on the lips is just praise for a job well done or something more. So, with this slate, they can say, "hey brother wanna go see
Green Lantern
and some other movie, they're doing a double header?" Takes away all the pressure. By the time the house lights come on after
Brokeback Mountain
, if they're both flushed, that's great. Ultimately, he just wants to know if they're on the same team.
Mary is in utter disbelief. Her eyes well up and she wipes them, blushing heavily.
MARY
(voice crackling)
Haven't we learned our lesson about double headers?!
She quickly picks up the grocery bag and the assorted items that spilled and walks out of the theater. After a few moments of stunted silence, Connie runs out of the theater to catch up with her.
WARREN
Feels pretty predatory. I'm not comfortable with exploiting today's gay youth for profit.
(he ponders)
On the other hand, it is a nice night out for two boys filled with curiosity and a couple bucks to spare.
No one responds to his comment.
ROBERT
(breaking the silence)
But why are all these films Uranian? I'd imagine quite a few girls on the softball team may want a crack at this too.
NATHAN
You are absolutely correct, though I wouldn't necessarily call them Uranian. Besides, the fact is women are
much
more perceptive at that age. In any four season city, young girls automatically know "a beanie past May means you're absolutely gay."
ALAN
He's right, my niece... we were on Coney Island in August and she just refused to take the damn thing off.  Sure as a black night's sky, she brings a girl to Thanksgiving.
No one responds. Nathan continues putting up the posters. Jimmy eyes the door, then looks to Robert and Warren, contemplating a walk-out.
Charlotte, who was watching from the stairwell, comes into the lobby.
CHARLOTTE
Give him a chance, we can't be doing any worse.
JIMMY
I'm a little worried about having a bunch of boys around here with The General, he might round up Dorothy's friends and start a platoon.
DAKOTAH
Ah, yes. That was the other announcement. The payroll just can't sustain a full staff, and, well, you tell me who should go instead of him.
The crew look to Alan.
WARREN
Isn't he's a volunteer?
DAKOTAH
In business, he's what we call a
liability.
ALAN
(meekly)
It's what we need to survive. The greater good. He'll be alright.
The General walks into the doorway with his pail and broom.
THE GENERAL
Attennnntion.
(he stays in a salute)
The room is silent. The General looks at the crew's faces.
Jimmy walks over and gives him a big hug and a pat on the back.
JIMMY
(sincerely)
Thank you for your service. You've been dismissed. We've won the war.
The General is in disbelief.
THE GENERAL
(tears streaming down his face)
Has Saigon fallen?!! I can't believe it.

heading: EXT. MARY'S HAMPTONS HOUSE - EARLY MORNING
content:
Mary sits alone on a stone bench in the garden of her parents’ lush Southampton home. Bedraggled with purple crescents beneath her eyes, she smokes a cigarette and swigs from a near-empty bottle of red wine.
She hears the gravel of the long driveway CHURN as a 90s Range Rover pulls up to the front of the house.
She watches as her father, RICHARD, early 60s, grey hair, but in great shape for his age, exits the house to approach the car. He is dressed in white shorts and a white polo, carrying a tennis racket and bag.
Mary sees a man step out of the car, also wearing white shorts and a white polo. They embrace. Neither look over to the garden where Mary is seated.
The two men have a short, inaudible discussion, before the visitor quickly hops into the driver seat of his car, grabs his racket, and removes the keys, before joining Richard in his Mercedes G Wagon.
As they leave, they stop close to Mary and roll down the driver's side window, revealing Dakotah in the passenger seat.
RICHARD
We have a doubles match against the O'Briens. We'll be back for dinner.
The two smile and wave as they pull off. Mary waits until the car has left before she drifts over to the pool. There are several empty wine bottles scattered near the sun-beds. She peers at her reflection from the pool’s edge, then turns away and lets her body fall backwards into the water, holding out her cigarette to protect it. Like a corpse she floats lifelessly with her eyes closed, taking an occasional drag.
Her mother, FLORENCE, early 50s, well-dressed and well-mannered, casually approaches the pool. She is completely unfazed by Mary being in the water.
FLORENCE
Daddy is inviting Dakotah to stay for dinner, the Clifts are out East. I called William, he's bringing his new wife. So be ready by seven.
Mary flips over to be face first in the water. Florence walks away.

heading: INT. MARY'S HAMPTONS HOUSE, DINING ROOM - NIGHT
content:
A long dining table sits in the middle of a tastefully decorated dining room. Seated at the furthest end of the table is Richard, with Florence to his left and Mary next to her. Mary has clearly attempted to clean herself up after a long day of drinking, but her anguish shows. To Richard's right is WILLIAM CLIFT JR., a handsome man in his 50s, followed by TATYANA, mid 30s, Russian and covered in jewelry. Dakotah is to her left, directly across from Mary.
The table is jammed with terrines of food and empty plates, interspersed with wine and champagne bottles. Everyone is sated, apart from Mary who’s perched solemnly on the periphery of the conversation, absently playing with a full plate of food in front of her as the others talk. As the dinner progresses, Mary consistently puts back glasses of wine.
WILLIAM CLIFT JR.
(laughing)
What can I say, love is love.
He puts his arm around Tatyana.
FLORENCE
Aww.
She puts her hand over Richard's, warmly.
DAKOTAH
Now every time I come out East, it's pierogi's and borscht, but honestly, it's not bad.
MARY
(to William, curtly)
What's an all-American guy doing with a soviet Elizabeth Taylor? Flo can give you our psychiatrist.
FLORENCE
(fierce)
Marianne how dare you!
TATYANA
(in Russian, to Mary)
I'm a spy.
WILLIAM CLIFT JR.
(cracking up)
She says she's a spy!
The group except Mary laugh mightily.
Richard stands to pour more champagne into Tatyana's glass. He looks to Dakotah.
RICHARD
How are you managing at the theater, regretting it yet? At this point, the land is worth more than anything inside. Could do a full demo and build something more befitting of the times. Maybe a food court?
FLORENCE
Yes! After we dropped Mary off, Richard was starved. We went to one of those "Taste the World" cafeterias in Downtown Brooklyn. It was so authentic. A group of homeless came in when it rained, but I'm sure they'll sort that out in time.
DAKOTAH
Ah I don't think that's what I'm after. The theater is iconic. Last resort, we'll have to try for historical landmark status.
RICHARD
Now that's clever.
Mary rolls her eyes.
DAKOTAH
If all goes well, the only food available will be stale popcorn and Red Vines. Just in these last few weeks getting to know Alan, I think he'd rather die broke than see the hard hats come in.
RICHARD
I'd be weary of that type of thinking. Every man has his limits.
MARY
They've come before. We don't need Dakotah's business wizardry to turn the Four into another beer hall for Brooklyn yuppies.
FLORENCE
I'm sure Dakotah has a plan.
WILLIAM CLIFT JR.
I'll make a call.
DAKOTAH
Dad! No. We don't need a call.
RICHARD
Dakotah, your privilege is your power. Never be ashamed of what your family can do for you. They earned it. You got that job on merit, right? Now use what's in your arsenal and help this poor man and his theater.
DAKOTAH
Actually, it was Charlotte who introduced me to Alan.
Mary's eyes widen.
MARY
Fuckin' slimy bitch. I knew this was all too convenient.
FLORENCE
Oh little Charlotte Ladue! She was priceless. I must call her mother and set up dinner. Mary, do you remember when you used to babysit, and you couldn't stand her?
RICHARD
(in between gulps of whisky)
Mary - Mary used to say, "for $17 dollars an hour, I'd rather be epileptic."
(bursts out laughing)
You've changed so much honey... ahh maybe it was Mount Holyoke...
Mary pushes her chair away from the table, finishes her glass of wine in one chug, and walks away, out the door, shutting it with force. There's an awkward silence.

heading: EXT. MARY'S HAMPTONS HOUSE - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Mary is standing in the foyer in front of the house, staring out into space. A cigarette is in her mouth, unlit.
She hears the front door OPEN and FOOTSTEPs, as Dakotah cautiously approaches her.
He offers her a light, she leans forward and accepts it, taking a drag with the cigarette tip in the flame. Once the cigarette is lit, she looks at him and exhales a drag. She puts the cigarette back to her lips with a shaky hand. Cigarette in mouth, she lets out a whimper and a cough. She continues coughing, which morphs into a full-blown sob.
She jerks away and stumbles onto the gravel driveway, leaving Dakotah on the porch. He looks on with concern as she heads to a Mini Cooper, parked amongst the fleet of expensive cars in the driveway.
DAKOTAH
(calling out)
Mary, what are you doing?
She doesn't stop or turn around and continues to wobble toward the car.
DAKOTAH
(scream)
Mary!
MARY
(blubbering scream)
Oh fuck you!
She gets into the car.
As Mary turns on the engine, Dakotah rushes over to the car and stands in front of it. Putting his hands on the hood.
DAKOTAH
(loudly)
It's going to be okay.
From inside the car, Mary has turned on the RADIO loud enough to drown out what Dakotah is saying. She can only see his mouth move, as the RADIO drowns out everything he says.
MARY
Get out of the fucking way! Leave me alone!
Dakotah sees Mary yelling but can't hear anything over the music. She eventually gives up, rolls down the window, and sticks her head out.
MARY
Don't think I won't run your ass over.
Dakotah circles to the driver side window and bends down to be eye level with Mary.
DAKOTAH
First you'd have to turn the engine on.
MARY
It is on you fucking idiot.
DAKOTAH
No, your battery is on.
(a pause)
Can we just talk outside? We can figure this out. Come on out.
Dakotah tries the door, it's locked.
MARY
Stop that.
Mary presses the break and goes to push the car's start button. As she does, Dakotah reaches into the car and grabs her arm before she presses it, holding it firmly.
DAKOTAH
(very stern)
Mary, you're wasted. Get out right now. I will go get your parents.
Dakotah keeps his hand on Mary's arm. She uses her other hand to try and pry it off.
MARY
(appalled)
Now you're attacking me?
DAKOTAH
Mary.
MARY
(screaming)
Get off!
Dakotah's demeanor changes, and he lets her arm go and backs out of the window.
DAKOTAH
Mary, I'm going to get Richard.
Dakotah quickly heads back towards the house. As soon as he's out of the way, Mary, tears streaming down her eyes, puts the car into drive and begins peeling away.
Dakotah turns around and screams after her, and takes off following her.
DAKOTAH
Do not! Are you crazy?
She speeds down the driveway and turns into the street, erratically.
Dakotah runs the length of the driveway trying to catch up with her. He stops and looks out at the direction Mary drove away.

heading: INT. CHARLOTTE'S APARTMENT - NIGHT
content:
Zev and Charlotte are sitting on her couch, close to each other. Charlotte's apartment is a loft in the West Village, immaculate in decoration, with old furniture, lots of plants, and large windows.
ZEV
The neighborhood is beautiful, I still don't know how you leave this for Brooklyn.
He leans back toward the end of the couch and sees a framed photo of Gustav Klimt's
Portrait of Hermine Gallia.
ZEV
You look just like her.
Charlotte looks over to what Zev is referring to and breaks into a wide smile.
CHARLOTTE
(sarcastically)
My great-grandmother.
Zev does not pick up on the joke. She leans over and lies on him as they both look at the photo.
CHARLOTTE
(perking up)
What do you want to watch?
ZEV
You're
asking
me
?
CHARLOTTE
Mmmm.
(she looks over at her closet)
Let me see.
Charlotte stands up and walks over to her closet. She begins opening it then stops and turns to Zev.
CHARLOTTE
So tell me something. Did you really like those Rohmer movies or were you just trying to impress me?
ZEV
(jokingly)
No, no, I just enjoy sitting for an hour and a half on the subway to see you.
CHARLOTTE
You can be honest, I just want to know what you like!
ZEV
I'm being honest! I really liked them!
CHARLOTTE
Yeah?
She turns back to her closet and fully opens it, revealing shelves and shelves of DVDs.
CHARLOTTE
(pondering)
Okay then... so you like European stuff... which you should, because they're the best... hmm...
While Charlotte is scanning the shelves, Zev gets up, shocked, and walks to the closet.
ZEV
Whoa, whoa, whoa, wait, what is this? You've got all of world cinema in this closet!
She turns to Zev and smiles.
CHARLOTTE
You can be honest, is this a red flag?
ZEV
No, I love it.
(in awe)
This is passion.
CHARLOTTE
(turning back to the shelves)
Anyways, maybe I should show you something Italian.
ZEV
We did have pizza for dinner.
CHARLOTTE
You need Fellini.
She takes
8½
out from the shelf.
ZEV
Okay.
CHARLOTTE
He's like the most famous of the Italians. Him and Antonioni, but
his
stuff is kinda slow.
CHARLOTTE
Or...
(looking through the shelf and pulling out a DVD)
Nights of Cabiria
. It's one of his early ones, too.
ZEV
How did you get all these?
CHARLOTTE
I've been collecting movies since middle school.
ZEV
You've been into film for that long? I'll be dead before I watch half of these.
CHARLOTTE
Well, growing up I alway spent summers in the Hamptons, and I didn't have many friends back then. Not that I do now, but my only really close friend spent her summers in the south of France. So we wouldn't see each other until school started again. One year, we started watching the same movie on Monday, write a letter about what we thought, and by the end of the week, we'd send letters back. It made the summers less lonely.
ZEV
At twelve years old your preferred movies were black and white French New Waves about sexual
(mispronouncing)
ennui?
CHARLOTTE
Not quite. It started with everyday movies, then moved into Hollywood classics, and eventually my dad suggested we try some foreign films and it just grew from there.
There's a moment of silence.
ZEV
Are you close with your dad?
CHARLOTTE
Very.
ZEV
Got it. Well this is really... something.
Charlotte smiles and brushes past him with the DVD in hand and heads to her DVD player, while Zev heads back to the couch.
ZEV
So, does everyone who comes over get a tour of the closet?
CHARLOTTE
Zev, I don’t just have anybody over to watch Italian movies. Is that what you think of me?
ZEV
I don't know!
CHARLOTTE
I'm offended.
ZEV
No, no, no, it's not that! I'm sorry. Really. I'm just... here you are, with a movie closet in West Village... I'm in uncharted territory, that's all. I feel like a shmuck.
CHARLOTTE
I like that about you. You're not like most guys I meet, trying to prove they know the world. And you're not a shmuck, you're just figuring things out.
There's a pause.
ZEV
What are most guys like? Dakotah?
Charlotte bends down and begins fiddling with the DVD player.
CHARLOTTE
What is that supposed to mean?
ZEV
Opportunistic, maybe? Hellbent on destruction?
CHARLOTTE
(ribbing him)
Are we a little jealous?
ZEV
I don't know... I mean, does he seem like somebody that really cares about art to you?
Charlotte, still bent over, turns her head to look at Zev.
CHARLOTTE
I don't know. I think he cares about keeping the theater alive, and that should be the bottom line.
ZEV
I get a weird vibe.
Charlotte stands up and starts walking back to the couch.
CHARLOTTE
Maybe he'll turn the place around and that way people will be able to enjoy the cinema for years to come, or discover it for the first time.
Charlotte sits back down on the couch, close to Zev, turns towards him, and begins running her fingers through his hair.
CHARLOTTE
Let's wait and see. He might turn the place around, you never know. And besides, let's not talk about work tonight. Let's just enjoy the movie.
Charlotte gives Zev one last quick kiss.
Zev kisses Charlotte on the head, then reaches over to switch off the lamp next to the couch while Charlotte clicks play on her remote.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - NIGHT
content:
The theater is unusually lively with pairs of teen boys waiting for the screening room to open for
Midnight Cowboy.

heading: INT. THEATER BATHROOM - CONTINOUS
content:
Robert, Mary, Jimmy, Connie, and Zev are packed in closely in the bathroom. They are speaking in low voices.
Mary's arm is in a cast; she has a busted lip and a black eye.
MARY
If Jean Luc Retárd makes us run
Mission Impossible,
I'm going to burn this place to the ground.
CONNIE
(quietly, shocked)
Mary...
WARREN
He's pretty cute.
CONNIE
He doesn't look at all like Montgomery.
MARY
He's going to turn us into a Regal.
JIMMY
Shhh!
FOOTSTEPS are heard approaching the bathroom. Robert turns the light off. The FOOTSTEPS subside.
MARY
(whispering)
Charlotte, I can't believe she set this whole thing up.
ZEV
What?
MARY
They were high school sweethearts.
Zev's heart sinks.
ZEV
I didn't know they knew each other.
Jimmy embraces Zev, who is confused by the gesture, busy being preoccupied by this new information.
A second later, the bathroom door swings open. Dakotah steps in and switches the light on.
CONNIE
AHH!
Dakotah YELLS and jumps back.
DAKOTAH
Jesus, you scared me!
(collecting himself)
Now, I don't want to overstep my boundaries but time theft costs corporations 57 million dollars a year, and we're out here fighting for pennies! We need to work together, this isn't me versus you, it's us keeping the theater alive. Can I count on you?
The crew are silent for a moment, looking to Mary, who gives Dakotah a stern, silent stare. They take this as a signal to hold their position against him and they file out of the bathroom. Mary is at the back of the group.
Dakotah puts his hand on Mary's shoulder to stop her as she attempts to pass him and leave the bathroom with the rest of the group.
DAKOTAH
(low voice)
I'm not going to say a word.
MARY
Should've just left me.
DAKOTAH
You could've really hurt yourself.
MARY
At least I wouldn't have to bear what you're doing to the Four.
DAKOTAH
(frustrated, whispering)
