Can you get a grip?! You know better than all them, but you act like I'm a devil trying to shut you down.
MARY
You aren't?
DAKOTAH
(exasperated)
Jesus.
Mary pushes past him, leaving Dakotah alone in the bathroom.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - MOMENTS LATER
content:
The crew is filtering into the lobby from the bathroom.
Jimmy holds Zev back for a private chat.
JIMMY
Zev, can I tell you something about trust?
ZEV
(annoyed)
Sure.
JIMMY
There was a point in my life when I did therapy twice a week, for $250 dollars an hour. That's $2,000 dollars a month, Zev. And you know what I did? I don't even know why, but sometimes I just couldn't stop myself.
ZEV
I don't know if I really want to hear this.
JIMMY
I lied. I was filthy liar. At first it was little things like "oh yeah, no I'm stuffed" when I had in fact not eaten at all. Then, one day, my therapist asked if I liked the Mets, because I was talking about living in Crown Heights. I've never watched a baseball game, I don't know the rules, but my mouth just said, "yeah, I've been a fan my whole life."
(pondering)
Maybe it's my subconscious wanting to really be from Brooklyn.
(sternly)
Well it turned out that she'd watched them religiously since childhood with her father.
ZEV
(ironically)
Oh no.
JIMMY
I had to commit. I learned RBI's, Curveball, Umpire, Barry Bonds did steroids, a pitch killed a bird. I watched
Bull Durham
. I watched all the
Seinfeld
episodes where George worked for the Yankees; the cotton uniforms! Every week I would have to look up the scores because all our sessions began to start with 5 minutes of baseball chat. For nine months I did that. Do you grasp the weight of that statement; how much money of the little money I earn that I've spent talking about the Mets?
ZEV
I do. I really do.
JIMMY
(serious)
It's a sickness, there has to be something seriously wrong with me. I'm 30 years old. I travel, on the subway, to an office, to sit across from a licensed psychotherapist, whose only job it is, is to accept me, unconditionally and without judgement. And I still can't do it Zev. I just can't.
ZEV
(still annoyed)
That's quite a story.
JIMMY
Anyways, what I'm trying to say is, a lie can be a lie, or it can be a truth untold... Do you understand what I'm trying to tell you? I wouldn't get involved unless I cared. We all do. You're our concessionaire.
Zev stares at Jimmy for a moment, then glances at the stairs.
Jimmy turns to walk away and Zev slips up the stairs to the projection booth.

heading: INT. PROJECTION BOOTH - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Charlotte is sitting in the projection booth when she hears a KNOCK on the door. She goes to open it, revealing Zev.
CHARLOTTE
Hey!
ZEV
(curt)
Hey. How's it going?
CHARLOTTE
Good, good.
Charlotte is taking a DCP out of the projector.
ZEV
(making small talk)
So,
Grease
, huh?
CHARLOTTE
Yeah, I don't think
Pulp Fiction
fits into Nathan's grand plan.
ZEV
You know, Dakotah was talking about his Dalton days earlier. That's where you went right?
CHARLOTTE
Was he?
ZEV
(a touch aggressive)
You never told me you knew each other.
CHARLOTTE
Why is that suddenly such a big deal? Do you want my whole resume? Mary went to Dalton too if you want the whole picture.
ZEV
You weren't fucking Mary, or were you?
CHARLOTTE
(hurt)
Zev!
ZEV
Why wouldn't you tell me? The other night, we were talking all about him...
CHARLOTTE
Neither of us had a prom date but it wasn't anything romantic, we were kids. And when did my past become your business? Worry about the present. Who am I fucking now?
ZEV
Me, but I'm not entirely sure I'm the only one.
CHARLOTTE
Don't be a jerk! I thought he could help the theater, I spoke to Alan... and it's working! Does no one care about how there's actually people here tonight?
ZEV
You've been so defensive of the guy... what am I supposed to think?
CHARLOTTE
(getting upset)
Nothing! I've known men before you, I hate to break that headline. I'm allowed to have people from my past, friends or otherwise, that I still keep in touch with.
Zev starts to get worked up and becomes visibly angry.
CHARLOTTE
You're spending too many nights dancing in the theater 'til sunrise, lapping up every word they tell you.
ZEV
Must be a Dalton thing, I wouldn't get it.
CHARLOTTE
(upset)
Why are you doing this to me?
ZEV
I'm not doing anything to you. You lied to me!
CHARLOTTE
I didn't lie!
ZEV
(overlapping)
By omission! A truth untold!
Charlotte begins to cry.
ZEV
You go on and on about cinema, the art, and you let it all go to waste because you want to do a favor for some hotshot who doesn't know Antonini from... from
Fast and Furious.
CHARLOTTE
You don't have to be an asshole. That's just not true. This place was being run into the ground. Plus, I don't want to hear it. You wouldn't know who any of those people were if it weren't for me and--
ZEV
You're right! And that's why I'm shocked! I thought of all people
you
would understand why what we do here is important. But hey, what's it to you? Place goes under, it's just another opportunity for daddy to come in with the bulldozers.
CHARLOTTE
Zev, what in the fuck are you talking about? Also don't bring my dad into this.
There's a tense pause.
CHARLOTTE
(trying to defuse the situation)
Maybe Dakotah'll save this place, he'll save it and more people will discover great film.
ZEV
How? Where? One super villain movie at a time? Where's the art?
Charlotte is at a loss for words.
ZEV
Look, I know you love him but the rest of us...
CHARLOTTE
Oh God, you asshole. Fuck you. You know what, I do have a history with him. We hooked up during summers in college, and I didn't want to tell you, because I thought those days were over. I thought we could just have something together, but now you're Sherlock Holmes'ing my past like I ever betrayed you? Honestly, get out of my booth and get out of my life.
Zev storms out. Charlotte is dumbstruck, unsure if she made the right decision turning the tables on Zev. She quickly pushes the thought away, locks the booth, and loads a reel for
Midnight Cowboy
.
As the film begins, she falls into her chair, tearing up.

heading: INT. THEATER OFFICE - AFTERNOON
content:
The entire crew is gathered in the theater office, with Alan, Dakotah, and Nathan up at the front. Zev and Charlotte are seated on opposite ends of the room.
NATHAN
(reading from a notebook)
9% growth week over week for the last month. Concessions up 14%.
DAKOTAH
We are on pace for something really big, and it's only going to get better.
MARY
Going from four people to a dozen isn't really a breakthrough.
JIMMY
It has been starting to buzz a bit, I can't lie. Strangely it hasn't even been mostly teens, it's like dads who golf and Hell's Angels.
NATHAN
Yeah, I noticed this as well. I built an adjustment into my model and we're adding
Magic Mike XXL
to the slate.
Mary doesn't even flinch; she's numb to it now.
DAKOTAH
Growth is growth, and the best is yet to come. And I've noticed you all like to hang out after work, so to thank you, I'd like to treat the group to drinks tomorrow night at my place.
Alan is beaming.
ALAN
Show some appreciation for Dakotah everyone. We're turning a new leaf!
CONNIE AND WARREN
(deflated, under their breaths)
Elaine May.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - LATER THAT NIGHT
content:
The double header has finished and pairs of older biker men and middle aged white-collar dads file out of the theater; one pair flush red and holding hands, the others not as successful.
Zev is behind the counter with Connie still perched near the ticket station, despite the lack of customers. Warren, Robert, and Jimmy are nowhere to be seen.
Connie looks in Zev's direction. Zev replies with an exasperated expression.
CONNIE
Poor fellows.
ZEV
(distraught)
They gave it a shot.
(a pause)
I'm not going to come to Mary's, I need a little extra rest tonight.
CONNIE
Me too, actually. You take the G, right?
ZEV
Yeah.
CONNIE
I'm headed in that direction, too.

heading: EXT. BROOKLYN STREET - NIGHT
content:
Zev and Connie are walking together on the sidewalk.
CONNIE
You know, growing up outside of New York, you don't really realize that people just exist here. You think of it as such an alien lifestyle.
ZEV
What do you mean? I exist here. You're existing here.
CONNIE
I don't know, it's just different when you're on the outside looking in.
They walk a few paces in silence.
CONNIE
I love walking on nights like tonight. When it's balmy, when the air takes on a sort of lightness, almost.
Connie smiles a little.
ZEV
I guess that's what you meant by some of us just existing here. Brooklyn is Brooklyn, it's never been magical to me.
(pause)
Where are you from, again? I'm sorry, I realized I totally forgot.
CONNIE
I don't think you ever asked. I'm from Memphis--well, Germantown, actually. It's a suburb.
(perking up)
I love it, don't get me wrong, but it's definitely not enchanting.
ZEV
(lighthearted)
Not many guys like me over there, I bet. I'd like to visit one day, if you ever go back.
CONNIE
(she nods)
Definitely. I miss my family a lot.
(motioning to the stop)
Anyways, this is where you get on, right?
Connie and Zev arrive at the train stop.
ZEV
Yeah, but you know what I could go for? An ice cream sandwich. They come in a two pack, if you're not one of those lactose people.
CONNIE
Yes! I don't know if you wanna walk this far, but the bodega across from my place has the Magnum Doves.
ZEV
(considering, eyeing the subway)
I don't mind a stroll.
CONNIE
Are you sure? I don't want you to have to walk all the way back.
ZEV
It's no problem!
(trying to be clever)
I too love walking on nights like tonight.
The pair begin walking down the street, away from the stop.
CONNIE
Sorry if I'm dragging my feet, I go into my other job tomorrow and I hate it.
ZEV
(surprised)
Two jobs?
CONNIE
It's at a little art studio in Long Island City. I was supposed to be working on craft books and pottery kits but it's proven to be way more corporate than artistic, which is the last thing I want to be involved with.
ZEV
I had no idea you were into pottery.
CONNIE
Well... What I really want to do is be the person that comes up with the drawings for coloring books. For adults.
ZEV
I've never heard of someone coming to New York City to be an adult coloring book-ist.
CONNIE
I guess I just thought if I was gonna find my future, I was gonna find it here.
ZEV
A lot of people seem to think that, but take it from a local, I'm not so sure about all that.
(a pause)
I should really get back into coloring, it's been years probably. My colors always bled between the lines.
CONNIE
I just think people don't take time to appreciate the beauty of the world around them.
Connie waves her hands, motioning to the quiet, empty street.
CONNIE
But when you're coloring, you're really focusing on it.
ZEV
And Cinema Four is sort of your halfway house to drawing full time?
CONNIE
I think so. I didn't really mean to get involved at the Four at all. One day I walked by and saw they were running a Studio Ghibli marathon. I came in, watched them all, and then Mary noticed me hanging around and asked if I wanted to apply.
ZEV
How long do you think you can take tickets before you're all dried out?
CONNIE
It really depends. I think about it a lot. I don't think I could go back to Germantown. Maybe Memphis, Nashville... Or maybe somewhere totally different, like out west.
ZEV
I never had that feeling growing up, that I had to go somewhere else. I could just take the G to Fulton and I was there. Everything is here, so why leave?
CONNIE
That's why everyone comes here and pretends to be a local, right? It's the cliche. But I wouldn't trade growing up in Germantown for the world. I remember being a little girl, and being in my room late at night, on a Saturday night where I didn't have school the next day, and I'd hear a train way off in the distance. And that, and maybe the hum of a car way off, would be the only things I'd hear. And it would all seem, you know, the place where the train was, far off--it would all seem so mysterious and I'd wonder where the train was headed. And I'd feel so unbound and free. In New York, I know it sounds silly, but the train you hear is the subway, and you know exactly where it is and where it's going. There's no mystery, or at least not anymore. And I don't know if I wanna know everything all the time.
(pause)
I'm sorry, I probably sound so stupid right now.
ZEV
No... I understand you.
Connie stops walking at her building, Zev keeps going a few paces before realizing. He looks back.
CONNIE
(lightheartedly)
Here she is.
ZEV
Oh.
CONNIE
But you know what I just remembered? I actually have some Talenti in my freezer. Any interest in just having that instead?
There's a pause.
ZEV
Okay. Sure.
Zev walks behind Connie and follows her up the steps into her building.
A light flicks on on the third floor.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - THE FOLLOWING NIGHT
content:
Mary, Warren, Connie, Robert, and Zev are in the lobby hanging out. Dakotah, Charlotte and Nathan walk in from the screening room.
DAKOTAH
(to everyone)
You guys ready to head out? It's in Manhattan, which I know can seem treacherous.
MARY
(rudely)
We can't make it tonight, but any other time just let us know.
There's a silence.
WARREN
Rob and I are meeting with a big producer tonight, otherwise we'd love to...
CHARLOTTE
(to Zev)
Zev, really?
Zev looks to the others and exchanges a look with Connie.
Dakotah walks over and wraps his arm around Zev.
DAKOTAH
Four's company to me. We'll catch you all another night.
The four of them walk out of the theater.

heading: INT. DAKOTAH'S APARTMENT - AN HOUR LATER
content:
Charlotte, Nathan, and Zev are in Dakotah's posh apartment living room.
Dakotah is in the kitchen getting drinks together. He walks into the living room with a tray of drinks.
DAKOTAH
Zev, I heard some great things about you. You've got good taste.
Dakotah winks at Zev suggestively.
ZEV
(curt)
Thank you. Charlotte, you don't live too far from here.
CHARLOTTE
No, I do not.
Dakotah hands out drinks to the group.
NATHAN
When you see Denis Lavant on screen and the way he moves his body, I mean it's pure magic. I'd take him over Peck or Dean or any of those guys.
(taking his drink)
Thanks.
Nathan sips his, expecting a mocktail given he does not drink.
He SHRIEKS.
NATHAN
Jesus fucking Christ!
He drops the glass and spills it all over his lap.
Zev and Charlotte are surprised.
Nathan looks at the drink Charlotte is holding.
NATHAN
My Shirley Temple!
DAKOTAH
Nathan, I'm so sorry, I must've mixed them up in the kitchen.
Nathan starts HEAVING. He runs to the bathroom.
DAKOTAH
We're going to order up a Pedialyte!
(to Charlotte)
I'll make you a new one.
CHARLOTTE
Did I take the wrong drink? I didn't mean to.
DAKOTAH
(heading to the kitchen)
No, no, that was on me.
Dakotah leaves the room. It's just Zev and Charlotte. It's silent for a few moments.
CHARLOTTE
How have you been?
ZEV
Why did you make me come?
The sound of a SHAKER can be heard in the back.
CHARLOTTE
Look, I don't want to hurt you.
ZEV
Just leave me alone.
Dakotah comes back in with a new glass and sits across from them. Zev and Charlotte play nice while Dakotah is around.
DAKOTAH
Cheers! To good company, and better business.
They CLINK their glasses. Dakotah enjoys his sip immensely and then puts down his glass and brushes his legs.
DAKOTAH
(in a low voice)
I'd like to tell you something Zev, if you promise to keep it between us. Only Nathan, Alan, Charlotte, and myself know.
Charlotte shoots Zev a look.
CHARLOTTE
(to Dakotah)
I don't think you should. Nothing's official.
DAKOTAH
Relax. We're amongst friends. This is great news, Zev will be over the moon.
ZEV
(eyeing Charlotte)
Whatever you feel comfortable with.
DAKOTAH
Ever heard of the Paris theater?
ZEV
I've never been to Europe.
Dakotah is momentarily confused.
DAKOTAH
(smiling slyly)
Netflix, that's all I'll say.
CHARLOTTE
That's enough Dakotah.
Dakotah downs his whisky and AAHs in delight.
ZEV
Yeah... I've heard of it.
DAKOTAH
All I'm going to say is Hulu could be interested in premiering films in
their own
theater.
ZEV
The Four? I love the place, but it's kind of a dump.
DAKOTAH
You'd be surprised what a paint job and new management does to a place.
Dakotah winks.
CHARLOTTE
Dakotah, enough.
DAKOTAH
I've got a couple of suits coming to do a walk-though. It's supposed to be incognito, so play it cool.
CHARLOTTE
Shut it! He’ll tell all of them before dawn.
DAKOTAH
Zev wouldn't do that. We have a bond. Plus, nothing at the theater will change. Actually most corporate acquisitions leave the business exactly as is, save for a few operational efficiencies. The idea that corporate takeovers somehow kill the culture that existed is a falsehood. You would all get raises, benefits, there would be big premieres.
CHARLOTTE
I wouldn't be surprised if they stage a lock in.
DAKOTAH
(starting to slur his words)
If they botch this deal, they're going to have another thing coming. At least in the
60s
,
the hippies were against a war. The only thing Mary is against is crossing the East River. And for what? Because Manhattan is nice? Do kids in the South Sudan rest their heads on their pillows to dream of coming to America to live in a roach infested four bedroom in Ridgewood, Queens? No, God damn it! They dream about the Empire State Building, Central Park, brunch at Ladurée. This...this poor chic aesthetic, it's
complete crap. If you were actually broke you'd be in Hoboken. And for some reason, everyone east of Williamsburg is an artist. When did being a regular person with a job become some sort of grand failure; a disgrace to humanity that we don't make paper-mache sculptures of our genitals?
ZEV
(sheepishly)
Actually, the male genitalia is vastly under represented in art. Mary is working on a photo series about it... It's called the Phallic Fallacy.
DAKOTAH
The fallacy is that anyone gives a damn.
(to Charlotte)
Did I do anything to offend them? They've been against the idea of me before I ever stepped foot into that lobby.
(to Zev)
Really, is what I'm trying to do so goddamn evil?
ZEV
I-I don't think so. The theater's dying, right?
DAKOTAH
They hate the idea of someone like me actually helping them. Is it because of who my grandfather is? Where my family comes from? Would they rather I slum it in Flatbush and
pretend
I don’t have a trust fund? They'd start calling me Mary.
CHARLOTTE
(stern)
Dakotah, relax.
ZEV
They just want what's best for the Four.
CHARLOTTE
Do they really?
DAKOTAH
(to Zev)
Let me ask you something. You're a straight fellow
,
right? Do they look down on you for being a cis hetero, Plain Jane living life with no flavor?
ZEV
No I haven't felt that at all. I actually invited them all over for dinner at my parents' in Bensonhurst. They seemed to enjoy that a lot, and it's no Brooklyn-chic or whatever you're saying.
DAKOTAH
(beginning to really slur his words)
Ah, so they approved. You're a "real Brooklyn guy", that's a hall pass, congratulations.
(a pause)
You know what, I bet you think you got me figured out. Yeah, I'm rich and I'm handsome and I'm Montgomery Clift's grandson, but I'm no dickhead. You know where I was last night? While you were drinking Pabst and talking schmeers, I was in that very chair and I watched
8½
six times yesterday. And that's not even a record, ya dumb bitch. And let me tell ya another thing, Charlie Kauffman blows. Guy fuckin' sucks. We've all read books--well, some of us, maybe all those used bookstores in Bushwick are for show.
Nathan is VOMITING in the bathroom down the hall. Dakotah turns back toward the bathroom.
DAKOTAH
(loudly)
You okay, buddy?
(to Charlotte and Zev)
On top of everything, he's a celiac.
NATHAN (O.S.)
(slurring)
I can't... I can't see...
CHARLOTTE
Poor thing.
Dakotah stands up, walks over to his sound system, and turns it up to drown out Nathan's hurls. MUSIC starts playing.
ZEV
I'm going to check on him.
Zev stands up and leaves the room.

heading: INT. DAKOTAH'S BATHROOM - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Zev is standing over Nathan, who has his head halfway into the toilet bowl. Zev is patting his back.
NATHAN
I'm fine. Thank you my friend.
Zev empties a cup from the vanity and fills it with sink water.
ZEV
Here, rinse.
Nathan gurgles the water and spits it out.
NATHAN
(taking a deep breath)
I'm alright.
Zev slides to the door of the bathroom and peers into the living room.
He sees Dakotah holding Charlotte's hand, rubbing it affectionally. Zev doesn't react. He returns to Nathan's side.

heading: EXT. CONNIE'S APARTMENT - LATER THAT NIGHT
content:
Zev scans the buzzer list and then BUZZES 4C.
CONNIE (INTERCOM)
Hello?
ZEV
It's me.
She BUZZES him in.

heading: INT. CONNIE'S APARTMENT - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Connie waits at the doorway, distraught. Zev climbs the stairwell and rushes into her apartment.
Zev quickly takes off his coat and his shoes.
ZEV
(out of breath)
He's gonna sell the place.
CONNIE
Huh?
ZEV
I was at Dakotah's, he told us.
CONNIE
You're not making any sense!
ZEV
Dakotah is getting Hulu to buy the Four.
CONNIE
Whoa, what... what do they want with it?
ZEV
I have no clue, but they're huge. It'll probably ruin everything.
CONNIE
Zev, calm down. There's nothing we can do about it now. We'll tell the gang tomorrow and figure it out.
ZEV
I ran over here, I didn't know where to go. I had to tell you. Charlotte knows, and they all know.
Zev looks up at Connie, clearly distraught.
CONNIE
It's okay.
ZEV
I don't think I've ever felt this way about someone.
They embrace.
ZEV
(quiet)
I love you.
Connie leads Zev over to the couch and sits him down.
CONNIE
Try to relax.
Connie goes to her fridge and brings Zev a beer. He takes a big swig.
ZEV
(defeated)
What a fucking night.
(pause)
I know I'm probably overreacting. It's just, I...
(pause)
I can't paint, I can't play an instrument, I...
(starting to tear up)
I've never had any outlet for expression and I finally found something that made it feel like I was doing something other than just existing. And now it's going away.
Connie takes a seat next to Zev. He wipes away his tears as she turns on the TV and scrolls through various streaming options, skipping over Hulu.
Ace Ventura: When Nature Calls
beings to play.
Connie scoots closer into Zev and he embraces her. They break into smiles at Jim Carrey's whimsical performance.
TELEVISION (O.S.)
Do not fret, my little woodland friend, for your life is in the hands of Ace Ventura... pet detective.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - AFTERNOON
content:
Afternoon sun bathes the theater lobby, which seems atypically lively. It's a Saturday, so the full staff is working. Connie is in the ticketing position and Zev is behind the counter. The front doors swing open and Dakotah walks in with two men dressed in suits tailing him.
The crew take instant notice of the SUITED MEN and exchange tense glances.
DAKOTAH (O.S)
(to the suited men)
She was built in '32--the Golden Era of theater construction. I'm sure you're well aware of where this place stands in cinema history. They premiered
Break
fast at Tiffany's,
it was the local haunt for all the big names back then
.
Obviously, now we're in a bit of a slump, but with a little help...
Mary and Jimmy look at Zev. Zev nods.
Dakotah approaches the concession stand with the suits.
DAKOTAH
(to the suits)
Can we get you something? Zev here is the best in the business.
ZEV
(polite)
Hello.
SUIT #1
We're alright.
SUIT #2
(to Dakotah)
Tuesday though, can you make sure you have the melted cheese machine up and running
?
I can't sit through a 'flick without it.
DAKOTAH
(laughing)
I know what you mean.
Dakotah gives Zev a look.
ZEV
Sure thing. What's on Tuesday?
DAKOTAH
These gentlemen here are going to help us do a little test run. And if things are in working order, eventually a premiere.
SUIT #1
(jokingly)
We have to make sure that projector up there still runs.
DAKOTAH
On that note, let's head up to the booth.
Dakotah points to the stairs leading up the balcony and motions to the suits to head that way.
DAKOTAH
I'll meet you guys right over there in just a minute. I just have a question to ask Zev here.
The suits walk off, leaving Dakotah and Zev alone.
DAKOTAH
Zevvy, buddy, I'm sorry. Sometimes I hit the hooch a little too hard and go off the rails a bit. But you heard what I said, right?
ZEV
Yeah, Charlie Kauffman blows.
DAKOTAH
Be that as it may, I'm talking about all the other stuff. Now again, let's keep what's going on between us, but I wanted you to be in the know. You're a smart kid, you get it. We're cool, right?
ZEV
Yeah, we're cool.
DAKOTAH
Alright.
Dakotah nods to Zev, then turns to meet the suits at the bottom of the stairs.
DAKOTAH
(to the suits)
Sorry about that gentleman. Anyways, Charlotte'll give you the rundown... she's really what keeps this place running.
Dakotah leads the suits up the stairs to the balcony.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - NIGHT
content:
The crew sit together in the lobby somberly, drinking wine. Zev sits close to Connie.
JIMMY
(to Zev)
What exactly did he say the other night?
ZEV
(a bit flustered)
He asked me if I had ever been to some Parisian theater and talked about Netflix... I had no idea they were showing up so soon.
JIMMY
And at the stand?
ZEV
Dakotah said Tuesday they're running a test premiere, then they went to talk to Charlotte.
Mary, who had has been staring off at the theater walls, not very engaged in the conversation, speaks aloud, not directly to the group, just to herself, loud enough for the group to hear.
MARY
If any one of you comes to watch a movie here after the takeover, I'm going to punch you in the liver until you die.
JIMMY
Are you nuts, we would never.
MARY
Then you're worry-free.
JIMMY
Maybe now I'll have time to focus on my poetry.
MARY
You've worked here for eight years, your job mainly consists of walking past this lobby every three hours, watching movies whenever you want, and handing us an envelope with our money twice a month. Do you really think that's what been holding you back from your poetry career?
CONNIE
Mary, be nice! He's being optimistic.
JIMMY
No, she's right. I'll be job hunting anyway. I interned at the public library when I just moved to the city, maybe they need a general manager.
ZEV
Mary the murderer and Jimmy the balding library cop.
(to the group)
This is
our
theater. You're not even going to try and stop him?
MARY
What're you gunna do? Chain yourself to the screening room?
ZEV
I don't know! Sabotage the deal, send Dakotah packing back to Dartmouth.
MARY
Yeah, okay Danny Ocean.
ZEV
Yeah, why not?! Ask yourself what we have at our disposal.
The group stares at Zev in silence.
ZEV
Art! The single most powerful tool against a profit-driven, megalomanic society.
The crew stares on, taken aback by his zeal.
ZEV
Let's show them what artists are made of. Robert and Warren, they've been making movies for ages now.
JIMMY
Correction, they've been
talking
about
a
movie for ages now.
ROBERT
Oh shut up, Secret Dante.
ZEV
Charlotte showed me how to use the projector, I can get her out of the booth and put on our own movie. We'll show those suits what they're destroying.
CONNIE
I'll help.
ROBERT
Warren and I can provide the equipment and aesthetic sensibilities. Dante?
JIMMY
Fuck it, it's not gonna make a difference but might as well go out with a bang.
The crew looks to Mary, who is the only uncommitted member. She looks at Zev.
MARY
Sure, I'll join this revolution...
Mary fishes a platinum American Express out of her pocket and holds it up to the crew.
CONNIE AND WARREN
Robin Hood!

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - NIGHT
content:
It is after closing hours at the theater. The lobby is still as Alan, Dakotah, Nathan, and a dozen SUITED MEN, two of them the same men from the day before, exit Alan's office.
The crew is shooting nervous glances at each other as they watch the business people waltz through the lobby.
ALAN
(excited)
No, really, thank you for coming, it means a lot. I have to run home to feed the cats, but let's talk tomorrow, and Dakotah and I will sync. And then hopefully we get the lawyers to fill in the dotted lines.
The business men shake Alan's hand.
SUIT #2
A pleasure. Thanks for having us.
Alan touches their shoulders warmly. He exits the theater.
DAKOTAH
(to Nathan)
Go get these gentlemen some nachos and...
(to the business men)
Drinks? We've got the hard stuff if you're interested.
SUIT #1
Just a round of Sprites.
Nathan nods and heads to the concession stand.
NATHAN
Hello, Zev.
ZEV
Hey, Nate. How are you feeling?
NATHAN
Quite alright now. Did you see the executives?
Dakotah ushers the business men into the screening room as the crew watch them pensively.
ZEV
Yes, I did. What are they screening?
NATHAN
We're not allow to talk about it... Nachos, please.
Zev turns around to prepare the nachos when Robert comes behind into the concession stand area. He has a messenger bag over his shoulder.
ROBERT
(acting pleasant)
Ahoy, Nathanial.
NATHAN
Robert. Did you see the executives?
ROBERT
Sure did.
Robert bends down to "grab a snack" and leaves the messenger bag down there.
ROBERT
I'll catch you guys later.
He leaves.
ZEV
(with the nachos)
Okay... nachos. Anything else?
NATHAN
Sprites for everyone.
Zev nods and turns to the soda machine as Jimmy comes into the concession stand.
JIMMY
I'll finish these out and bring everything in. Can you make sure the cubby area is tidied up with Connie? We need to be showing our best.
ZEV
Sure thing.
He ducks down and subtly grabs the messenger bag.
He feigns toward the cubby area, and as Nathan is distracted holding Sprites and nachos, Zev slinks up the stairs toward the balcony.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, BALCONY - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Zev, shrouded in darkness, is quietly in the balcony area, out of view of the projection window. He is nervous.
He peers down at the orchestra to see the business men and Dakotah all seated and Nathan entering the room with their snacks.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - CONTINUOUS
content:
Dakotah is sitting between the two suits as suit #1 takes in his environment.
SUIT #1
This place really is beautiful. What'a fuckin' gem you found us.
SUIT #2
(to Dakotah)
Imagine this place packed - the balcony. Standing room only. This happened because of you.
SUIT #1
(turning to Dakotah)
You're an extremely impressive young man.
The suit shakes Dakotah's hand.
Dakotah receives it proudly like a cadet from his general.
The group sits back, staring forward at the screen, as the movie should be starting.
Without looking away from the screen, Suit #2 subtly passes an envelope into Dakotah's jacket pocket, at which point he turns to Dakotah.
SUIT #2
(winking)
Finder's fee.
Dakotah feels the touch of the envelope on his chest and it feels like the reception of a purple heart.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, BALCONY - CONTINUOUS
content:
Zev knocks on the projection room door. The bag is to the side, out of Charlotte's sight.
ZEV
(whispering)
Char.... Charlotte. We need you.
Charlotte opens the door a crack.
CHARLOTTE
(whispering)
Leave me alone!
ZEV
(frantic)
Wait -
(holding the door from being closed on him)
Dakotah sent me up here. He - he asked me - the executives asked him - he needs to know...
(unsure)
How many cans of film do we have left? I can't remember it was something technical. He said run up and ask you and I messed up and I don't remember.
CHARLOTTE
(mad)
Wait. What? That doesn't make any sense.
ZEV
(nervous)
I can't remember! He needs to know now. They want it before the movie starts.
CHARLOTTE
You're really something...
Charlotte hastily exits.
ZEV
Okay...
After holding the door open for Charlotte, Zev slips into the projection booth. He locks the door, surveys the booth, drops Robert's bag, and begins fishing through it.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Charlotte is speed walking up the aisle to Dakotah's row. Dakotah is surprised to see her.
DAKOTAH
What are you doing? We should be starting now!
CHARLOTTE
Zev said -
The projector starts up, Dakotah and Charlotte peer up. The business people have no idea something has gone astray.
After a countdown reel, an image forms on the silver screen:
"Mary Von Erb Presents"
"A Film By Robert O'Brien Love"
"Shot by Warren Gilles"
"Written by James S. Smith"
"Art Direction by Concetta 'Connie' Wong"
The sound of popcorn POPPING plays over a black screen.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - NIGHT - ON SCREEN
content:
The film fades into an image of the Cinema Four lobby, with Warren, Mary, Jimmy, and Connie all scattered in different parts of the lobby, staring directly at the camera. The camera pans forward, the scattered crew turning their heads to keep their eyes locked on the camera as it passes.
The POPPING morphs into a more ominous sound as the camera reaches the bottom of the stairs leading to the projection booth. The camera begins mounting the stairs, clearly carried by hand. No time to stabilize in post. The image fades.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, BALCONY - MOMENTS LATER - ON SCREEN
content:
Zev walks through the door leading to the balcony, and begins walking slowly down the balcony aisle steps.
ZEV (V.O.)
They say films are our collective memory, our subconscious, if you will. Then what about the movie palace?
Zev reaches the railing of the balcony and looks down. He puts his hands on the railing, grips them tight, then begins to lunge himself off.

heading: EXT. NEW YORK STREETS - ON SCREEN
content:
Archival images of old movie palaces being imploded play.
ZEV (V.O.)
What happens when a culture destroys its very own subconscious? We may soon find out. Gibbon theorized about how empires die. A glib corporation will show us how a culture dies.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, BALCONY - ON SCREEN
content:
Zev is hanging from the theater balcony, his legs flailing. He SCREAMS. A close up of his face shows perspiration and extreme anguish.
ZEV (V.O.)
The last gasps of a way of life.
The camera pans from Zev's face to the expanse below, where Warren, Mary, Jimmy, and Connie are standing, pointing and laughing inaudibly.
ZEV (V.O.)
While the so-called shakers and movers laugh.

heading: INT. THEATER OFFICE - ON SCREEN
content: Robert, dressed in a cheap suit and smoking a cigar, sits in Alan's office chair. He LAUGHS and stands up, puffing smoke, as Mary, dressed in a satin blouse and a long khaki skirt walks into the frame.

heading: INT. THEATER OFFICE - MOMENTS LATER - ON SCREEN
content:
Mary is reclining on the office desk as Robert begins untying her blouse, the room now filled with smoke. He continues laughing.
ZEV (V.O.)
The rot of industry. Has it always been a cancer? Has it always leached upon cinema? Why, we must ask, did we build the palaces in the first place?

heading: INT. THEATER OFFICE - LATER - ON SCREEN
content:
Robert is now supine on the desk, Zev standing over him, reigning blows upon him with a film reel. Robert cowers and attempts to use his arms to protect his face.
ZEV (V.O)
But the little guy will always stand tall.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - LATER - ON SCREEN
content:
Again we see Warren, Mary, Jimmy, and Connie standing still, staring at the camera.
WARREN, MARY, JIMMY, AND CONNIE
Whose side will you be on?

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - CONTINUOUS
content:
The house lights raise slightly, the room is dim but horrified faces are visible.
The executives look at Dakotah with indifference; they'll find another theater to buy. They get up silently and file out to the aisle, and out of the screening room. Two of the executives carry a third who's passed out in shock.
SUIT #4
(to Dakotah)
He always passes out when he watches something that sucks, but it hasn't been this bad since
Anomalisa
. Expect a medical bill.
In the very back rows of the theater, Robert, Jimmy, Warren, Connie, and Mary sit victoriously. They are all clapping and hollering in celebration. Robert leans back in his chair, having finally seen one of his films on the big screen.
Dakotah enters a rage and Charlotte sobs, in shock at what has just happened.
DAKOTAH
What the hell is wrong with you people?!
(walking out of the row)
This is insanity. This is pure evil. You worthless degenerates! You hipster scum fucks, you have no respect for anything in this world.
Dakotah rips the envelope to shreds and storms out of the theater.
DAKOTAH
I was going to save this dump!
Charlotte runs out chasing him, in tears.
Dakotah stomps back into the theater and looks at the crew.
DAKOTAH
And Mary, your Mother Theresa, you know where she'll spend this weekend while you all file for unemployment? In Southampton, drinking a glass of claret with the so-called
shakers and movers
.
Mary's face is flush red with embarrassment.
Zev stands in the projection booth, blank faced.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - NEXT MORNING
content:
Following a night of raucous fun by the crew, the screening room is in disarray. Empty bottles of alcohol, snacks, confetti, and kazoos cover the floors and chairs. The only light comes from the projector, which was left running with nothing to play.
The crew is scattered across the orchestra seats, passed out.
At the far end of the theater, a crevice of light forms as the entrance is opened. Alan walks in.
Silently, emotionless, he scans the room and absorbs the scene. Then he turns and exits, back into the corridor and out of the screening room.
The SLAM of the screening room door awakens Zev, with Connie unperturbed, still leaning up against him in slumber. In a groggy state he too scans the wreckage of last night's celebration. He softly moves Connie's head from his chest to the back of the chair and slinks into the aisle. He saunters to the screening room exit.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Zev peeks his head out of the screening room door to find Alan slowly walking through the lobby, looking around at the walls and displayed artifacts.
Zev is careful to stay unnoticed, unsettled by Alan's reaction, or rather non-reaction. Alan's back is to the screening room door, as he looks at the posters lining the wall.
ALAN
You're going to need a new job.
Zev, having expected anger, comes fully out of the door.
ZEV
Me, sir?
Alan, still talking away from Zev, starts walking to his office.
ALAN
(fatherly)
Come, let's see who I can call.
Zev is confused, but gingerly walks after Alan to his office.

heading: INT. THEATER OFFICE - MOMENTS LATER
content:
Alan is at his desk when Zev walks through the door. Seeing his face clearly for the first time in the light, it is evident that Alan has had a rough night. His face is sunken, eyes dried out.
Alan motions for him to take a seat. Zev sits timidly.
ALAN
Do you like salad, Zev?
Zev, nervous and confused, does not compute the question.
ALAN
Do you like salad. Be honest, do you like your greens?
ZEV
Not really, no... I'd rather have a burger.
Alan sighs deeply.
ALAN
Me too, kid. Unfortunately, Silver Screen Salads only serves one thing. They offered me a 10% stake if I didn't renew the lease. You're not offended if I don't feel inclined to recommend you and your friends to new management?
Zev thinks Alan's completely lost his mind.
ZEV
Are you okay?
There's a long silence.
ALAN
Do you think I do this for the money?
(pause)
I started working concessions here in '76, in high school. Then I was the projectionist, and  then the manager, which paid my way through college. After college, I left to work in sales. A few years later this place was basically a dump. Duncan, the owner, passed away in '88, and on the day of his funeral I stopped at TD Bank and took a loan against my apartment for $30 thousand dollars. His kids wanted nothing to do with the place, I probably could've gotten it for half that. It's been 33 years. I gave her my heart and soul and the lights never went out. They've been dim, but never out.
Alan, proud, triumphant, pauses.
ALAN
What do you want to do with your life, Zev?
ZEV
This is... was... I enjoyed working here.
Alan throws his arms up in purposefully satirical protest.
ALAN
You poured melted butter!
ZEV
(quick retort)
Yeah, but it was fun! Jimmy and Mary, they all took me in; I found out about Godard, Antonioni, Coppola. I never knew movies were art. It's cooler than anything. I feel it just sitting here.
Zev sits back in his chair, a look of exasperation on his face. Alan notices Zev's realization.
ALAN
(playfully)
Charlotte ever run
The Last Picture Show
for you? She loves that movie.
Zev looks at Alan, confused.
ZEV
No, she didn't.
ALAN
That's a damn shame. You'll never get to see Cybil Shepard, 64 feet wide, with your back pressed up against the velvet with an ice cold Coke, absolutely tented. Not in Bensonhurst.
Alan pauses.
ALAN
Take it from a fellow Brooklyn kid: when you're young, you can go around being content that you’re smarter than everyone. When you get a little older, you’ve gotta prove it.
Alan leans back in his chair, looks up to the ceiling in thought.
ALAN
Maybe I would've done it too.
(pause)
But probably not. Charlotte told me the movie sucked...
(a pause)
Maybe that was the point, they walked.
(perking up)
She did say the acting wasn't half bad. Then again, maybe Charlotte still has a soft spot for you.
(pause)
Do yourself a favor, do something with your life besides complain about what you don't have.
(pause)
Except a job, you're going to need one of those. Your world can be bigger than butter.
He gets up, and on his way out of the office pats Zev on his shoulder goodbye. Not lovingly, but decisive. Zev stays in his chair, looking lost.

heading: INT. ZEV'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
content: Zev is lying on his back, in bed, above the covers. All the lights are off and the curtain is drawn; the only light source is his fish tank. His eyes are wide open. He looks lost in thought.

heading: INT. ZEV'S APARTMENT, LIVING ROOM - AFTERNOON - DAYDREAM
content:
Zev's apartment is on a high floor of a glass building in Long Island City, overlooking Manhattan.
Shlomo and Sheryl sit closely on his couch, dressed up in their Saturday best. Their eyes light up as Zev, in a tuxedo, enters the room.
Shlomo springs up and embraces Zev with a hug.
SHLOMO
(so proud)
My boy. My boy. Shalom malom kitsuanu. Hashem leads you to the promised land!
Sheryl dabs her teary eyes.
ZEV
The car's here, we need to go.
She rushes toward the door, stopping to check herself out in the mirror. As Shlomo passes, she turns to tuck his shirt further into his pants.
SHERYL
You had to wear this shirt?!
ZEV
(from the hallway)
Let's go!

heading: EXT. ZEV'S BUILDING - MOMENTS LATER - DAYDREAM
content:
Zev, Sheryl, and Shlomo walk out of the lobby doors to the sidewalk.
Connie, window rolled down, smiles at Zev from the the back seat of a luxury Lincoln town car. She throws open the door and Zev and Sheryl get in the back, while Shlomo sits in the front passenger seat.
SHERYL
(to Connie, kissing her on the cheek)
Don't you look wonderful, Sweetie!
The car drives off.

heading: INT. THEATER LOBBY - LATE AFTERNOON - DAYDREAM
content:
The lobby is lit beautifully, the dying light of the summer afternoon pouring in. Alan paces around in the lobby, checking his watch.
Through the glass doors of the theater, he sees the Lincoln pull up to the curb. He rushes to the doors to meet them.
As Alan holds the door open, he ignores the four of them in order to berate the LIMO DRIVER.
ALAN
45 minutes late, you worthless son of a bitch!
Alan quickly forgets his anger and turns to embrace Zev lovingly.
ALAN
I'm so proud of you, Zev.
ZEV
I wouldn't have wanted it to happen anywhere else.
ALAN
It's packed.
(giddy, low voice)
I think I saw Rex Reed in the fourth row.
ZEV
Rex Reed?
ALAN
Only
the
biggest name in film criticism.
Alan turns his attention to Connie, Shlomo, and Sheryl, who are a feet few behind them.
ALAN
(to Connie)
Connie, dear, you look ravishing.
(to the Sterns)
And you must be Mr. and Mrs. Stern How proud you must be.
(ushering them warmly)
Let's head in to the theater... give the kid a moment.
Smiling, the four of them head to the theater. Zev, meanwhile, walks over to where the rest of the crew is gathered. Mary goes in for a hug.
MARY
My little artiste! Lay it on me! You know you still have to pick up some shifts every now and again.
WARREN
(interrupting)
Were those your parents over there?
ZEV
Yeah.
WARREN
Cute.
ZEV
I'll introduce you after the show.
ROBERT
Have you seen the theater yet?
ZEV
No, I haven't.
MARY
Oh you gotta go see it. The General got it all set up for you--it looks gorgeous.
The crew walks away and Zev begins to walk towards the screening room. On his way, he happens to glance up the stairs leading to the projection booth, where he sees Charlotte sitting, looking down, smiling. He smiles back and she winks. They look at each other for a minute, and then Zev continues on.

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - LATER - DAYDREAM
content:
The screening room is filled to the brim with well-dressed PATRONS. The General is in the back, broom and pale in hand.
Zev enters, and The General places a warm hand on his shoulder.
THE GENERAL
(completely coherent)
We're proud of you.
(looking out at the crowd)
Stay calm. You already did the hard work. Now just sit back, relax, and enjoy.
Zev looks down with a smile as The General nudges him forward.
Zev emerges into the audience's view. They immediately give him a standing ovation. Zev receives the praise with humility. He waves and takes a small bow as walks up the aisle to the row he sat in when he first watched
Breathless
.
He takes his seat next to Connie, followed by his mother and father, Alan, Mary, Jimmy, Robert, and Warren. The lights go down alongside the clapping as the film starts.
A series of title cards are presented on screen:
"With grant funding from the
Brooklyn Historical Society"
"Dedicated to:
the memory of a time and a place
"
"The Family Turst of Mary Von Erb Productions Presents"
"Directed by Robert O. Love"
"Based on an original screenplay by Warren Gilles"
"Zev Stern in"
"Cinema Five
"

heading: INT. SCREENING ROOM, ORCHESTRA - TWO HOURS LATER - DAYDREAM
content:
As the movie reaches its conclusion, all of the audience is crying.
ZEV (V.O.)
And that's what this theater is about. Not PROFIT, but ART.
Music wells up and the credits begin to play. The audience jumps up to cheer. Connie pounces on Zev with a big hug, and his mom pushes her out of the way so she can get a kiss in while wiping away a tear.
The pandemonium between Zev and his family calms down but the audience continues to clap. Connie looks him in the eyes then squeezes his hand. Zev looks forward in awe of the enthusiastic reaction. Connie pulls on his arm a little so he'll bring his ear down to her level.
CONNIE
You're gonna be rich!
Zev, looking a little taken aback, nods and gets a big grin on his face.

heading: INT. ZEV'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
content:
Zev, now sideways, is smiling when he snaps out of his day dream. He adjusts himself back to reality. He sits up in his bed and after a few moments of thought, he reaches over and grabs his phone. He makes a call.
ZEV
Hey it's me. Are you up yet?
A brief silence.
ZEV
Yeah. Anyway, I'll be over at Mary's in a bit, I'll pick you up.
A pause.
ZEV
(laughing)
No I'm not coming up, you're coming down! I hate when we show up an hour after everyone else - it's embarrassing.
A pause.
ZEV
Alright, see you soon.
Zev hangs up, gets out of bed, throws on his clothes, and fixes his hair in the mirror.
ZEV
(Italian style)
Ayyy.
He walks out of his room.
There is a newly taped-on
Saturday Night Fever
poster on the wall.
ZEV (O.S.)
(to Sheryl)
I'm taking the car. I might be late.
(Brooklyn Italian Accent)
Relax Ma, we're just goin' dancin'...
The front door opens and closes.
The ENGINE of Zev's car rumbles and ultimately fades into the distance as he drives away from the house.

heading: INT. ZEV'S CAR - NIGHT
content:
Zev is driving with Connie in the car.
They pass the location of Cinema Four and see construction workers with "Ladue Construction" vests. They have a group just set up the new marquee - "Silver Screen Salads".
There's a group protesting the building. One man stands on a crate with a megaphone. Zev and Connie HEAR him yelling "Cinema Four is a landmark in history, it must be saved."
Connie hangs her head out of the window as they pass.
CONNIE
I wonder if they're hiring.
Zev and Connie laugh, she rests his head on his shoulder as they drive. Cinema Salads in the rear view until it disappears into the distance.
